product sheet

benefits:

•	Static	and	Dynamic	Analysis

CAesAr ii®
intergraph® CAesAr ii® evaluates the structural responses and stresses of
piping systems to international codes and standards. it is the pipe stress
analysis standard against which all others are measured.

Data Input
CAESAR II makes it easy to input and display all the data needed to accurately define

a piping system analysis model. You can access or modify input on an element-by-

element basis, or select datasets to make global changes.

CuttIng-EDgE graphICs
The CAESAR II input graphics module makes quick work of developing analysis models

while clearly indicating areas of concern and providing an excellent idea of the piping

system’s flexibility. Color-coded stress models and animated displacements for any

•	Steel	Databases	and	Modeling

stress load case are available.

•	Expansion	Joint	and	Hanger	Databases

•	Hanger	Design

•	Bi-directional	Links	to	Intergraph

CADWorx®	Plant

DEsIgn tools anD WIzarDs
Tools and wizards for tasks such as creating expansion loops or viewing plant models

in the analysis space help bridge the gap between knowledge and experience. Such

tools take the guesswork out of producing accurate analysis and recommending

practical design changes.

analysIs optIons
Besides the evaluation of a piping system’s response to thermal, deadweight, and

pressure loads, CAESAR II analyzes the effects of wind, support settlement, seismic

loads, and wave loads. Nonlinear effects such as support lift-off, gap closure, and

friction are also included. CAESAR II also selects the proper springs for supporting

systems with large vertical deflections. Dynamic analysis capabilities include modal,

harmonic, response spectrum, and time history analysis.

Error ChECkIng anD rEports
The CAESAR II program includes an integrated error checker. This error checker

analyzes the user input and checks for consistency from both a “finite element” and

“piping” point of view. Reports are clear, accurate, concise, and fully user-definable.

MatErIal anD assEMblIEs DatabasEs
CAESAR II incorporates table look-ups for piping materials and components, plus

expansion joints, structural steel sections, spring hangers, and material properties,

including allowable stress. This ensures correct datasets are used for each analysis.

CAESAR II comes complete with major international piping codes.

bI-DIrECtIonal IntErfaCE WIth DEsIgn
CAESAR II incorporates seamless, bi-directional links between

plant design and engineering analysis. This allows the passing of

design and analysis between these workgroups without data loss.

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\Electrical Equipment Contract Packages.pdf
---

Develop Electrical Equipment Contract Packages - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Develop Electrical Equipment Contract Packages - Level 2

1.  As an electrical engineer, I need to develop the electrical equipment data sheets (Rev 1).
2.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to develop the instrumentation.
3.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to design instrumentation that requires electrical supply.
4.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to publish the electrical power element browser so that the power requirements of the instrumentation and cabinets can be included in the electrical design.
5.

If the document has publishable data, the document and the data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in
SmartPlant Foundation.

6.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to retrieve the electrical power element browser as published by SmartPlant Instrumentation.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Sharing Electrical Power Requirements between Instrumentation and Electrical

7.  As an electrical designer, I need to develop the electrical schematics (Rev 0) so that they can be included in the contract package which is sent to the vendors. For this, I need to develop a set of typical schematics that will serve as the basis for the

project’s electrical schematics.

8.  As an electrical engineer, I need to develop the single-line diagrams so that they can be used in the contract package which is sent to the vendors. The diagrams may also include three-line diagrams.
9.  As an electrical designer, I need to develop the power distribution board (PDB) layout drawings so that the power distribution board manufacturer can consider them as a basis for fabrication.
10.  As an electrical engineer, I need to develop the electrical equipment list so that I will be able to update the master equipment list.
11.  As an electrical designer, I need to develop the electrical I/O list so that I can include that information in the schematics and the wiring design.
12.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to publish the electrical I/O signal list report so that the instrumentation department can use this information to update their I/O assignment and wiring.
13.  If the document has publishable data, the document and the data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in

SmartPlant Foundation.

14.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to retrieve the electrical tags and equipment relations. This information can be used to further develop the I/O signal assignments, wiring, and loop drawings.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:
  Sharing I/O Signal data between Electrical and Instrumentation

15.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to design the electrical I/O assignment and wiring.
16.  As a member of the instrumentation department, I need to publish the electrical I/O so that it can be included in the electrical design. The published document will include the process interlocks and process I/Os.
17.  If the document has publishable data, the document and the data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in

SmartPlant Foundation.

18.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to retrieve the electrical I/O as published by SmartPlant Instrumentation.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:
  Sharing I/O Signal data between Electrical and Instrumentation

19.  As an electrical designer using a CAD package (such as SmartSketch) , I need to develop installation details based on customer requirements for the various electrical equipment that will be installed by the vendors or by construction and send them

as part of the electrical equipment package.

20.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to send the electrical equipment contract package to vendors so that they can start manufacturing or supplying the materials that will be needed for construction. This package may contain the following

documents:
  Electrical equipment datasheets and specifications
  Single-line diagrams
  Electrical schematics
  Power distribution board (PDB) layout drawings
  Electrical equipment list
  Electrical I/O list


Installation details

21.  As a vendor, I need to review the documents in the electrical equipment contract package so that I can start processing the order to supply or fabricate the electrical equipment.
22.  As a vendor, I need to provide certified fabrication drawings to the electrical department so that they can finalize the design. The drawings may include:

  Electrical wiring diagrams
  Three-line diagrams
  Panel fabrication drawings

Enterprise Work Process for Electrical Design

2

23.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to review the documents received from the vendor and update the design and documents of the electrical equipment contract package accordingly. Updates may be returned to the vendors, as

necessary.

24.  As an electrical designer using a CAD package (such as SmartSketch), I need to update the installation details with information returned by the vendors.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Electrical Design

3




# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\EWPforMTR.pdf
---

Enterprise Work Process for Master Tag Register Production - Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Managing Tag Numbers and Tag Registers

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Master Tag Register Production

1.  As a member of the Process department, I need to develop P&IDs so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

2.  As a member of the Process department, I need to publish the P&IDs so that the information is available to other disciplines.

3.  As an instrument engineer, I need to develop instrumentation so that I can specify the instruments required for the project.

4.  As a member of the Instrumentation department, I need to publish the instrument index so that the information is available to other disciplines.

5.  As an instrument engineer, I need to publish electrical tags so that the information is available to other disciplines.

6.  As an instrument engineer, I need to publish the I/O list so that the information is available to other disciplines.

7.  As an electrical engineer, I need to develop the electrical distribution so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

8.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish single line diagrams so that the information is available to other disciplines.

9.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish the electrical I/O signal list so that the information is available to other disciplines.

10.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish electrical schematics so that the information is available to other disciplines.

11.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish the cable schedule so that the information is available to other disciplines.

12.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish the instruments and cabinets report so that the information is available to other disciplines.

13.  As a member of the Electrical department, I need to publish the electrical equipment so that the information is available to other disciplines.

  This is a custom report.

14.  As an engineer, I need to create tags for the vendors in SmartPlant Master Tag Registry so that the vendor can provide the necessary data for the tags for which they are responsible.

15.  As an engineer, I need to generate register data lists containing tags that are formatted to capture the necessary vendor data for tag registers.

16.  As an engineer, I need to send the register data lists to the vendor so that he can input the prescribed data.

17.  As a vendor, I need to input the data prescribed by the register data lists so that I can supply the data on my tags to the EPC engineer.

18.  As a vendor, I need to return the completed register to the EPC engineer so that he can include data on my tags in the project’s tag registers.

19.  As an engineer, I need to import the data contained on the vendor’s tag registers into SmartPlant Master Tag Registry so that I can review the data in preparation for publishing it.

20.  As an engineer, I need to review the vendor data to ensure that it was completed correctly and includes all the required information.

21.  <decision point> As an engineer, I need to determine whether the vendor data is ready to be published.

22.  As an engineer, I need to publish the vendor data so that the information is available to other disciplines.

23.  As a 3D designer, I need to develop the 3D model so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

24.  As a 3D designer, I need to publish the 3D model so that the information is available to other disciplines.

25.  As a 3D designer, I need to publish the cable schedule so that the information is available to other disciplines.

26.  As a piping designer, I need to publish the isometric drawings so that the information is available to other disciplines.

27.  As a 3D designer, I need to publish the pipe spools so that the information is available to other disciplines.

28.  As a piping lead engineer, I need to develop the line list so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

29.  As a piping lead engineer, I need to publish the line list so that the information is available to other disciplines.

30.  As an equipment engineer, I need to develop the equipment data sheets so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

31.  As an equipment engineer, I need to publish the equipment data sheets so that the information is available to other disciplines.

32.  <automated task> The tag data contained in all the published documents is loaded into the publish domain for SmartPlant Master Tag Registry and consolidated into the Consolidated Data Warehouse.

33.  As an information manager, I need to review the tag data for readiness to generate the registers.

34.  As an information manager, I need to create the tag register documents so that I can monitor the status of register data collection and generate tag registers.

Enterprise Work Process for Managing Tag Numbers and Tag Registers

2

35.  As an information manager, I need to generate preliminary versions of the tag registers in Microsoft Excel so that I and others can review their contents. I will look for missing mandatory data and other data quality errors so that I can inform those

responsible for supplying the data to make the necessary corrections.

36.  As an information manager, I need to check data consistency across the SmartPlant tools so that I can check the quality of the tag information across all the disciplines. For more information, see the Check Data Consistency work process diagrams in

the Appendix (on page 4).

37.  <decision point> As an information manager, I need to evaluate the data quality of the tag data in SmartPlant Master Tag Registry and determine if the tag registers are ready to be generated for handover.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

38.  (Optional) As an information manager, I need to set new revision numbers on the register documents, if applicable.

39.  As an information manager, I need to generate new register document versions so that I can send them to the owner.

40.  As an information manager, I need to transmit the register documents to the owner so that I can satisfy the contractual requirements for data handover.

41.  As an information manager, I need to communicate data quality errors detected in the tag data to the proper sources so that they can be corrected.

Enterprise Work Process for Managing Tag Numbers and Tag Registers

3

Appendix

1.  Check Data Consistency - Instrumentation

2.  Check Data Consistency - Equipment

3.  Check Data Consistency - Piperuns

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\DocumentControl.pdf
---

Document Control Work Processes - Level 1 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Document Control

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Document Control Work Processes - Level 1

1.  As a client, I need to issue the project definition to the Document Control group for their information and for future use.
2.  As a document controller, I need to receive the project definition from the client so that I can set the document classifications and the document numbering system.
3.  As a document controller, I need to establish the project procedures so that the documents managed by SmartPlant Foundation can adhere to the client specifications.
4.  As a document controller, I need to issue the project procedures to external parties so that the subcontractors can also adhere to the same client specifications.
5.  As an engineer or sub-contractor, I need to receive the project procedures so that I can adhere to them while doing my work.
6.  As an engineer or sub-contractor, I need to create the project documents so that they can be used to design the plant or store information relevant to the design of the plant.
7.  As an engineer or sub-contractor, I need to perform my design work, making changes as needed, to prepare the document for submission to the client using document control.
8.  As an engineer or sub-contractor, I need to submit my document to Document Control so that they can review them. At this point, the documents are in the "working" state, meaning they have not been signed off yet.
9.  As a document controller, I need to check the documents from the engineer or sub-contractor to be sure that the meta-data conforms to the established project guidelines. This step may include adding the documents to the SmartPlant Foundation

system, if they are not already there.

10.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the documents need to be reviewed by others before being issued to the client so that I can send them to the applicable reviewers.
11.  As an engineer or sub-contractor, I need to review and comment on project documents that will affect my design so that my comments or revisions can be recorded for future use.
12.  <decision point> As a document controller, I need to determine if the documents need rework based on the comments made by reviewers.
13.  As a document controller, I need to sign off documents that have completed their review cycle, when applicable, so that they can be locked to prevent further changes until they are revised or until the client has provided comments. See the work

process Document Control - Document Check and Review - Level 2 Diagram (on page 3) for more information.

14.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the documents need to be issued to the client, for instance if they have reached a project milestone, so that they can be packaged for issue.
15.  <End1> If it has been determined that a document does not need further review, either internally or by the client, that revision of the document is complete.
16.  As a document controller, I need to issue documents to the client, identifying the reason for issue and any action that is needed, so that I can collect comments and feedback. See the work process Document Control - Issue Documents to Client -

Level 2 Diagram (on page 5) for more information.

17.  As a client, I need to receive the documents sent by the document controller, along with information about why the documents were being sent and what I am expected to do with them, so that I can provide the requested feedback.
18.  As a client, I need to review documents sent to me by the document controller so that I can verify their validity and send them back with comments if they need rework.
19.  As a client, I need to determine whether the documents are acceptable in their current state or whether corrections or changes are necessary.
20.  As a client, I need to tell the document controller that the documents have met with my approval and are accepted so that my approval can be recorded.
21.  <End2> If the client determines that the documents are acceptable in their current condition, that revision of the document is complete.
22.  As a client, I need to send annotated documents, or comments regarding the reviewed documents, to the document controller so that they can be reworked.
23.  As a document controller, I need to receive documents back from the client, so that client comments can be reviewed, and the documents revised as required.
24.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the client comments on the documents require revisions to the documents so that they can be sent to the appropriate departments for revisions.
25.  <End3> If it is determined that the client comments do not warrant change to the document (a new revision of the document), that revision of the document is complete.
26.  As a document controller, I need to route documents that need revisions to the appropriate departments so that they can be revised to include changes requested by the client.

Enterprise Work Process for Document Control

2

Document Control - Document Check and Review - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Document Control

3

Document Control - Document Check and Review - Level 2

1.  As a document controller, I need to load documents coming from the external subcontractors into the SmartPlant Foundation database so that they can be managed with the SmartPlant Foundation software.
2.  As a document controller, I need to check the properties of the documents and their titleblock information to ensure they follow the project procedures.
3.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the properties of the documents and their titleblock information follow the project procedures so that I can send them to the appropriate party for correction, if needed.
4.  As a document controller, I need to package a set of documents that require rework for distribution on a transmittal so that they can be sent to internal or external parties, identifying the reasons, actions required, and the due date. I need to group the

documents on the transmittal for different reasons and/or types, or create transmittals specifically for each document type. In many cases, the recipients can be configured based on the organization, document type, reason for issue and other
document data, but if necessary, I may modify the recipient list. The transmittal will have an associated transmittal letter. In some cases, this step many include signing off any document that needs to be included on the transmittal.

5.  As a document controller, I need to send the transmittal and related documents that need revisions in their properties or titleblock to the appropriate internal departments or subcontractors so that the data can be corrected.

  Steps 6, 7, and 8 below describe the process for internal engineers (in the same company as document controller) to correct documents, using SmartPlant authoring tools. The documents may be checked out, modified, and then checked back

in, if the environment does not include integration, or, if working in an integrated environment, the engineer can make the changes in the applicable authoring tool and publish the changes to SPF.

6.  As an engineer using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to receive a notification that documents need to be reworked so that I do not have to look for them.
7.  As a user of a SmartPlant authoring tool, I need to make changes to the documents related to the transmittal, based on comments from the document controller or markups made by document reviewers. These changes may include design changes

Intergraph Proprietary Information

that will be reflected on the document or they may be changes to the document meta-data (titleblock and document properties).

8.  As an engineer using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to route revised documents back to the document controller for review, using a workflow, package, issue request, or so forth.

  Steps 9, 10, and 11 below describe how internal engineers, using applications other than SmartPlant authoring tools, would correct documents.

9.  As an internal engineer with access to the SPF database, I need to check out documents from the SPF database so that I can make the required changes.
10.  As an internal engineer, I need to make the required changes to the document using the applicable, non-SPE application, such as Word, MicroStation, AutoCAD, or so forth.
11.  As an internal engineer, I need to check in the documents to which I made the applicable changes so that they are back in SmartPlant Foundation for document management.

  Steps 12, 13, and 14 below describe how engineers outside the organization, subcontractors outside the firewall, or vendors would correct documents.

12.  As a member of an outside organization (subcontractor outside the firewall, vendor, etc.) who is not using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to receive documents (on DVD, FTP, or any other method) that need rework so that I can make the revisions.
13.  As a member of an outside organization who is not using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to make changes to the documents based on comments from the document controller or markups made by document reviewers. These changes may include

design changes that will be reflected on the document or they may be changes to the document meta-data (titleblock and document properties).

14.  As a member of an outside organization who is not using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to send revised documents back to the document controller (on DVDs, FTP, or any other method), so that the changes can be stored in SmartPlant Foundation.
15.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the documents need further review by any internal department or subcontractor so that I can send them to the appropriate party for review.
16.  As a document controller, I need to package a set of documents that need further review for distribution on a transmittal so that they can be sent to internal or external parties, identifying the reasons, actions required, and the due date. I need to group
the documents on the transmittal for different reasons and/or types, or create transmittals specifically for each document type. In many cases, the recipients can be configured based on the organization, document type, reason for issue and other
document data, but if necessary, I may modify the recipient list. The transmittal will have an associated transmittal letter. In some cases, this step many include signing off any document that needs to be included on the transmittal.

17.  As a document controller, I need to send the transmittal and related documents that need further review to the appropriate internal departments or subcontractors.
18.  As an engineer using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to review the documents related to the transmittal and provide comments or annotations. These comments may be provided in the form of SmartPlant Markup redline layers, company-specific

forms, email notifications, and so forth, according to the organization guidelines for comment tracking.

19.  As a member of a subcontractor company who is not using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to review the documents, mark them up using whatever method is available to me (such as hardcopy redlining), and return my comments to the document

controller.

20.  As a document controller, I need to collate responses from the external subcontractors, who are not using SmartPlant Foundation, so that I can determine if additional changes to the documents are required.
21.  As a document controller, I need to determine if the documents require additional changes to them so that they can be routed back to the appropriate department or departments.
22.  As a document controller, I need to package up a set of documents that need further work for distribution on a transmittal, so that they can be sent to internal or external parties, identifying the reasons, actions required, and the due date. I need to
group the documents on the transmittal for different reasons and/or types, or create transmittals specifically for each document type. In many cases, the recipients can be configured based on the organization, document type, reason for issue and
other document data, but if necessary, I may modify the recipient list. The transmittal will have an associated transmittal letter. In some cases, this step many include signing off any document that needs to be included on the transmittal.
23.  As a document controller, I need to send the transmittal and related documents that need to be revised, in order to incorporate review comments, to the appropriate internal departments or subcontractors so that the corrections can be made.
24.  As a document controller, I need to sign-off any working documents, setting the status to "current," so that they can be issued.

Enterprise Work Process for Document Control

4

Document Control - Issue Documents to Client - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Document Control - Issue Documents to Client - Level 2

This process is based on the default transmittal workflows delivered with SmartPlant Foundation. The delivered workflows may be configured, as necessary.
1.  As a document controller, I need to sort the documents by type, organization, company, or whatever grouping is required so that I can package them into separate transmittals.
2.  As a document controller, I need create the transmittal object, which includes defining the reason for the issuance of the transmittal, specifying the external organization that will be the recipient of the document, and indicating the due date. If

necessary, I can specify a particular initiating department or individual who is sending the transmittal.

3.  As a document controller, I need to ensure that all documents that need to be included on the transmittal are signed off (status set to "current") and are attached to the transmittal objects that are being managed by the transmittal workflow in

SmartPlant Foundation so that they will be properly managed by the transmittal workflow object.

4.  As a document controller, I need to review the default recipients, as determined by the workflow or any matrix used, so that I can ensure that the transmittal is issued to the appropriate reviewers. This step includes, if necessary, setting the actual

recipients manually. Each recipient who will be receiving the transmittal should have a defined reason for receipt that indicates why they are receiving the information and what action is expected of them.

5.  As a document controller admin/manager, I need to review the list of documents included on the transmittal, the transmittal details (such as the due date), and the recipients of the transmittal to validate the information. Once this information has been

reviewed and confirmed, I need to approve the transmittal, and change its status to "issued," preventing further changes to the document or recipients lists.

6.  As a document controller, I need to export the transmittal structure so that I can distribute the associated documents to the reviewers. This automated process creates a package of the documents (and associated files) that are part of the transmittal

and places it outside of the SmartPlant Foundation environment so that it can be distributed to the required recipients. This process also creates the transmittal report (or "top sheet") that describes the transmittal (documents attached, reason for issue,
recipient list, reasons for receipt, and so forth).

7.  As a document controller, I need to send the documents to the client using an FTP site, a DVD, or any other method.

Enterprise Work Process for Document Control

5

Develop Subcontracts - Level 1

4.  As a subcontracting engineer, I need to complete the ITT package and present it to the project stakeholders and management for review and approval.
5.  As a project stakeholder, I need to review and approve the ITT package as presented to me by the subcontracting engineer.

the materials.

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\ICS_DefiningElectricalConsumersFromPIDs.pdf
---

Defining Electrical Consumers from P&IDs

August 2013 - Revision 1.6

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process
The P&IDs that include the process related electrical consumers and loads, including electrical motors and their driven process equipment, electrical heaters
and other equipment that require electrical power, are published. The P&IDs are retrieved in SmartPlant® Electrical to create, modify or delete the electrical
consumers. This data is used to create the Electrical load list (not all loads appear on the P&IDs, just the process related ones) and the design continues for
the electrical distribution, assignment of the loads to the various motor control centers, etc.

Business Value
  Use information that is already available from Process (on the P&ID) to improve consistency with the Electrical design. This improves transparency



between the two disciplines.
Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes on the P&IDs (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items), and by
deciding how and when to apply these changes in the Electrical environment.

  Reduce data entry for more accuracy and to save time.

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.
  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.
Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.


  SmartPlant P&ID, Smart 3D, and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment.
SmartPlant Electrical and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.
Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.
Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.



  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on eCustomer for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M products used in your

environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.



  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.
In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.
In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.
If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.
If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.







  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

SmartPlant P&ID

SmartPlant Electrical

equipment must be set.

SmartPlant P&ID

(e.g. tag uniqueness)

If using workshare, you must publish from the Host site; therefore, P&IDs
owned by a workshare satellite must be transferred to the host site. (5,8)

(e.g. tag uniqueness)

Publishing process timing and frequency
  Additions, deletions, and major updates to equipment data should be

Retrieving process timing and frequency
  NA

published as soon as they can be approved, because they are critical to
downstream disciplines.

  The project must plan for intervals when all the P&IDs owned by a

workshare Satellite are transferred to the Host site in order to publish the
P&IDs. Progress on the P&IDs should not continue at the Satellite (although
this is technically possible in standalone workshare) until the P&IDs have
been transferred back to the Satellite.    (5,8)

  NA

Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints apply  Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints apply
  Any equipment item in SmartPlant P&ID that has the value defined for the
  When using SmartPlant applications in an integrated environment, editing
Electrical Requirement property is recognized in SmartPlant    Electrical as
Other Electrical Equipment load item type.

objects in a document previously published, such as a P&ID, requires work
processes that consider the impact of your edits on downstream disciplines.
For example, traditionally, an engineer marks up a P&ID to move part of the
items to another P&ID. The mark ups communicate a need to delete the
items from the source P&ID and create new items on the target P&ID. In an
integrated environment, when the P&ID is republished, other downstream
applications that have previously retrieved the P&ID receive Delete and
Create tasks during the next retrieve. These tasks can unnecessarily impact
system performance and create more work for the downstream disciplines to
resolve the resulting inconsistencies.
If items need to be moved within the same P&ID, simply move them within
the drawing. If items need to be moved to another P&ID, do not delete them
from the source P&ID. Instead, use SmartPlant P&ID’s Move to Drawing
command to move the items to a new P&ID. This command preserves all
data, including internal UIDs.
You should disconnect objects to be moved from existing objects or break
the piperuns so that the area can be easily selected with a fence select.
Both drawings will need to be republished.



  After the motor and its associated equipment (i.e. pump) are created, you
can copy the Driver Power Absorbed value from the process equipment to
the Brake Power property of the motor, using the command Copy Driver
Power Absorbed.
If the motor is associated with Pump (subclass = “pumps”), or Other
Mechanical Equipment (subclass = “Other Mechanical Equipment”),    the
associated pump or other mechanical equipment is also retrieved into
SmartPlant Electrical.
If the heater is associated with vessels (class = “vessel equipment”), the
associated vessel is also retrieved into SmartPlant Electrical.



  Only exchangers that are associated to bayonet electrical heaters are

retrieved by SmartPlant Electrical.

SmartPlant Electrical

SmartPlant P&ID
  On the P&ID, you can place the motor for a pump (or any mechanical
equipment) explicitly as a separate component, or imply the motor by
placing a driven-label on the pump. Note that if the motor associated with
the pump is not explicitly shown on the P&ID, but indicated with a label, the
electrical requirement should be defined on the pump itself; in that case
SmartPlant Electrical will retrieve the pump as an Other Electrical
Equipment object (not a Pump), without creating a motor, and handle the
electrical requirement with that object.

  When using integration, only embedded P&ID border files are supported. Do

not use linked border files.

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\ICS_ElectricalPowerRequirements.pdf

Sharing Electrical Power Requirements Between Instrumentation and Electrical

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Electrical Power Requirements Between Instrumentation & Electrical

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process
In SmartPlant® Instrumentation, the Require Power Supply flag is set for instruments and cabinets that require electrical power supply, marking them for
inclusion in the Electrical Power Element browser, which is published and retrieved into SmartPlant Electrical. In SmartPlant Electrical, the instruments and
cabinets are created, updated or deleted in order to design the switch boards and circuits that will feed the instruments. The Instrument & Cabinet report is
then published and retrieved in SmartPlant Instrumentation, including the item tag of the feeding switch boards (a.k.a. Power Distribution Boards / PDB), the
bus and the circuit, which are used by SmartPlant Instrumentation for loop drawings and reporting.

Business Value



Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) in one engineering
discipline, and by deciding how and when to apply these changes in another discipline later in the work process

  Minimize data entry and duplication of work, resulting in less engineering hours and better data consistency

  Better and clearer communication across disciplines via exchange of data

  Bi-directional data exchange supports flexible workflows, adapting to the user’s own work processes

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.



Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Electrical Power Requirements Between Instrumentation & Electrical

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information



Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on the Intergraph Smart Support web site for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M
products used in your environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

Sharing Cable Data Between Electrical and 3D Design

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Cable Data between Electrical and 3D

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process

Some electrical equipment is shown on the P&IDs and placed in the 3D model from the published P&IDs, while other electrical equipment is placed directly
into the 3D model. The cable connecting these electrical items is designed in SmartPlant® Electrical, and the Cable Schedule report is published and
retrieved into Intergraph Smart™ 3D to manually route or auto-route the cables. After defining custom surface style rules in Smart 3D that change the color
of trays that are filled to a specified maximum capacity, identity the overfilled cable trays. Relocate cables from the overfilled trays, if needed.    Then, the
cable length, path, and routing status are published from Smart 3D and retrieved into SmartPlant Electrical.

Business Value



Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) in one engineering
discipline, and by deciding how and when to apply these changes in another discipline later in the work process.

  Manage cable data in a single environment for electrical engineers and re-use it in the 3D model, maintaining design consistency and data quality, and

avoiding data errors.

  Provide electrical engineers precise cable lengths, resulting in better cable sizing.

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.



Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.


Integration Capability Statements Sharing Cable Data between Electrical and 3D

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on eCustomer for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M products used in your

environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

basis to all 3D locations.

  The project must plan for intervals when all the P&IDs owned by a

workshare Satellite are transferred to the Host site in order to publish the
P&IDs. Progress on the P&IDs should not continue at the Satellite (although
this is technically possible in standalone workshare) until the P&IDs have
been transferred back to the Satellite.    (5,8)

  Additions, deletions, and major updates to equipment data should be

published as soon as they can be approved, because they are critical to
downstream disciplines.

  The deletion of tags (equipment, instruments, and so forth) that have been
published previously should result in the republishing of the documents
where they existed as soon as possible.

Other tools that participate in this integration on the retrieve side

Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints apply  Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints apply

  For versions prior to S3D 2011 R1 HotFix09, if the cable is connected to an
Instrument in SmartPlant Electrical, the cable is not retrieved (correlated) in
Smart 3D. This capability was added in S3D 2011 R1 HotFix09.

  When publishing the 3D model for retrieval into SmartPlant Electrical, you
can set a filter to publish electrical data only. This will significantly reduce
retrieval time in SmartPlant Electrical. (29)

  When using SmartPlant applications in an integrated environment, editing

objects in a document previously published, such as a P&ID, requires work
processes that consider the impact of your edits on downstream disciplines.
For example, traditionally, an engineer marks up a P&ID to move part of the
items to another P&ID. The mark ups communicate a need to delete the
items from the source P&ID and create new items on the target P&ID. In an
integrated environment, when the P&ID is republished, other downstream
applications that have previously retrieved the P&ID receive Delete and
Create tasks during the next retrieve. These tasks can unnecessarily impact
system performance and create more work for the downstream disciplines to
resolve the resulting inconsistencies.

If items need to be moved within the same P&ID, simply move them within
the drawing. If items need to be moved to another P&ID, do not delete them

  Smart 3D does not create nor correlate parallel cables from the Design
Basis. Parallel cables are currently only supported in non-integrated
environments.

  Ensure that all electrical equipment items that will be connected to cables

are included in the model before cabling can be started.

Using Instrument Dimensional Data to Drive 3D Design

  This work process, primarily involving SmartPlant® Instrumentation and Intergraph Smart™ 3D, can be driven with or without the P&IDs. The

recommended process is to use the P&IDs.

Work Process Diagram 1: Using instrument dimensional data to drive 3D design with P&IDs (Recommended process).

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Using Instrument Dimensional Data to Drive 3D Design

1

Work Process Diagram 2: Using instrument dimensional data to drive 3D design without P&IDs.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process

In Smart 3D, you can place instruments using one of two different methods: stock instruments defined in the Catalog, or non-stock (on-the-fly) instruments.
DDP integration only applies to non-stock instruments.

This work process enables the Instrument Engineer to have full control of all the instrument data required in the 3D model, including the dimensional group,
physical dimensions, as well as weight and connection properties. The Instrument Engineer specifies the physical dimensions for types of instruments in
SmartPlant Instrumentation. This is published to SmartPlant Foundation and retrieved to Smart 3D (in the design basis) where the item is available for

Integration Capability Statements Using Instrument Dimensional Data to Drive 3D Design

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

placement, either by selecting the instrument from the P&ID or, if the P&ID is not used, by selecting the tag number from a list of instruments in the design
basis. The 3D instrument is created based on the data provided by the Instrument Engineer.

Business Value

  Provides the ability to manage all instrumentation data in a single environment used by Instrument Engineers (SmartPlant Instrumentation) for re-use in

the 3D model, eliminating duplicate entries, which improves efficiency and data quality.



Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes to instruments (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) in one
engineering discipline, and by deciding how and when to apply these changes in another discipline later in the work process.

  Reduces the hours required by the 3D designer to locate and input the physical dimensions for instruments.

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.





Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on the Intergraph Smart Support web site for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M
products used in your environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Integration Capability Statements Using Instrument Dimensional Data to Drive 3D Design

3

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

  Nested symbols, such as a symbol for the main body of a control valve

  The instrument symbols are based on prominent instrument manufacturer
catalogs, including Rosemount, Endress+Hauser, Daniel, Brooks, and
Yokogawa. Likewise, the control valve and actuator symbols are based
on manufacturer catalogs, e.g. Fisher and Samson. The Smart 3D
symbols and the SmartPlant Instrumentation symbols (DDPs) have been
made available on the Intergraph Smart Support web site, which is
updated once a month. A bitmap is available for each instrument and
control valve symbol as a means of documenting the dimensional basis of
the symbol. If a suitable symbol is not available, the user can request that
a new symbol be developed at no cost to the user, if the following criteria
are satisfied: 1) the user provides the manufacturer’s catalog for the
instrument or control valve; and 2) Intergraph’s schedule for delivering the
symbol on the Intergraph Smart Support web site is acceptable to the
user.

whether the existing instrument was placed with or without DDP data:



instrument with the new DDP data.

If the existing instrument was initially placed without DDP data, use
the Design Basis Viewer to update the instrument with the new DDP
data.

tags, thus eliminating the need to remember and key in the tag number.

Smart 3D
The following only applies when using SmartPlant P&ID:

  SmartPlant P&ID 2009 Service Pack 4 and 2009 Service Pack 3 HF10
introduced the relationship OwnsComponentOcc (OCC) to correctly
assign component ownership at specification breaks. By default, this
relationship is not mapped between the SmartPlant P&ID map file
(SPPIDDataMap.xml) and the SmartPlant Schema.

  When publishing P&ID drawings for use by Smart 3D, you should
update the SPPIDDataMap.xml to include the OCC relationship. If
you do not include this relationship, the new auto-correlation in Smart
3D 2011 R1 and later places the piping component in the ambiguous
section of the auto-correlate dialog instead of assigning the
component to the correct piperun.

  However, when publishing P&ID drawings for use by SmartPlant

Instrumentation 2009 Service Pack 2 or earlier, you should continue
to exclude this relationship. The relationship may cause SmartPlant
Instrumentation to switch between the two piperuns at the spec break
each time the P&ID is retrieved.

  For what other purposes can this published document be used?      The instrument DDP published document is used for this integration work

process and the data and document are loaded into the SmartPlant Foundation repository for viewing and navigating data.    The instrument tag on the
DDP can be used for auto-correlation.    Once correlated, it is possible to navigate to the DDP document from other documents.

SmartPlant Enterprise Data Exchange flow diagrams and SmartPlant Adapter for SmartPlant Instrumentation Guide, SmartPlant Adapter for Intergraph
Smart™ 3D    Guide posted on the Intergraph Smart Support web site under Product Documentation.

  Does this integration support publish/compare in SPF (e.g. is it a likely candidate for interdisciplinary consistency check)?    The data
contained on the instrument DDP is tailored for providing the physical dimensions to construct the 3D model.    It is not a likely candidate for
publish/compare.



Is a graphic file published and is it hotspotted in SPF?    In work process 1 involving SmartPlant P&ID, yes, the P&ID is a graphic file and is
hotspotted.The DDP document publish includes a viewable file showing the physical dimensions; however, it is not hotspotted.

  Are there limitations on auto-correlation in SPF?    The instrument tag published with the instrument DDP can be used for autocorrelation as long as
it matches, exactly, the tags of previously loaded instruments. However, piping ports on instruments from Smart 3D and SmartPlant Instrumentation are
not auto-correlated in SmartPlant Foundation.

  What is the content being extracted out of the document container being retrieved?    Instrument physical dimensions, weight, and connection

properties.
