# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\ICS_IOSignalDataBetweenElectricalAndInstrumentation.pdf
---

Sharing I/O Signal Data Between Electrical to Instrumentation

August 2013 - Revision 1.6

  This work process can start with the creation of I/O signals (electrical tags) in SmartPlant® Electrical or SmartPlant Instrumentation, as shown in the

two diagrams below.

Work Process Diagram 1: Sharing I/O Signal Data, starting in SmartPlant Electrical

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Work Process Diagram 2: Sharing I/O Signal Data, starting in SmartPlant Instrumentation

Integration Capability Statements Sharing I/O Data between Electrical and Instrumentation

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

4.  SmartPlant Electrical (SPEL) uses the term “I/O Signal” and SmartPlant Instrumentation (SPI) uses the term “Electrical Tags” to represent the same

objects.

Short Description of Work Process
There are several possible work processes for sharing I/O Signals between the Electrical and Instrumentation disciplines.
1.

In the first work process diagram shown, the Electrical Designer creates the I/O signals and associates them to the main equipment items. The I/O List
report is then published for SmartPlant Instrumentation to retrieve as Electrical Tags (a.k.a. I/O Signals in SmartPlant Electrical) and relationships to the
equipment items. From the SmartPlant Instrumentation browser module, the user can view the Electrical Schematics to understand how the signals are
wired.    After the I/O assignment and the wiring are designed, the Electrical Tags are published, and then retrieved by SmartPlant Electrical with I/O
assignment as Card, Channel and Terminals information and the ESL loop name that can be shown in the various SmartPlant Electrical reports and
schematics.
In the second work process diagram shown, the Instrumentation Engineer creates Electrical Tags based on I/O requirements and designs the I/O
assignment and wiring. The Electrical Tags, with I/O assignment as Card, Channel and Terminals information, are published for SmartPlant Electrical to
retrieve. In SmartPlant Electrical, the user relates the I/O signals to electrical equipment, and the I/O List report is published, and the SmartPlant
Instrumentation user retrieves it to receive the equipment to electrical tag relationships.

2.

Business Value


Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) in one engineering
discipline, and by deciding how and when to apply these changes in another discipline later in the work process.

  Minimize data entry and duplication of work, resulting in less engineering hours and better data consistency.
  Better and clearer communication across disciplines via exchange of alphanumerical and graphical data.
  Bi-directional data exchange supports flexible workflows, adapting to the user’s own work processes.

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.
  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.
Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.



Integration Capability Statements Sharing I/O Data between Electrical and Instrumentation

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

  SmartPlant P&ID, Smart 3D, and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment.
SmartPlant Electrical and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.
Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.
Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.



  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on eCustomer for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M products used in your

environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.



  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.
In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.
In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.
If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.
If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.







  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

Integration Capability Statements Sharing I/O Data between Electrical and Instrumentation

  When publishing electrical reports, 3 factors affect which items are

published:

  The Report Item Type
  The Filter set on the Report
  The Report Items to Publish to SPF preference in Options

Manager’s General Settings (Publish “Main items only”, “Main items

  When running the “I/O Signal List with Equipment” report, you must have the

delivered EquipmentToPublish filter in your plant, even if you don’t
specifically use it.
If this filter does not exist in your plant, this is the filter criteria:

  Filter Name: EquipmentToPublish
  Definition: “Match Any”
  Property: AllowPublish =”Yes”
  Property: Item Type Name = “SignalRun”

  The “I/O Signal List with Equipment” report has the Report Item Type “Plant
Item” which has the potential to publish a very large amount of data if it is
not filtered effectively, especially when publishing with the “Main and All
Related Items” setting.
Do not use compound filters as they currently do not give expected results.

Short Description of Work Process
Instruments can be created in three environments:
1.
2.
3.
After the instruments are created, their process data is defined in the SmartPlant Foundation Process Domain, and the Data Sheets are published, and then
retrieved into SmartPlant Instrumentation, where the instrumentation design continues.

In SmartPlant® P&ID, as shown with the path starting with "Start 1"
In the SmartPlant Foundation Process Domain (or IPD, for Instrument Process Data), as shown with the path starting with "Start 2"
In SmartPlant Instrumentation, as shown with the path starting with "Start 3"

Business Value
  The SmartPlant Foundation Process Domain application provides a structured and easy-to-use Microsoft Excel environment for Process Engineers to
create and manage instruments and their process data. Process engineers can update and publish the process data and the instrument engineer
receives a notification that the data sheet has been revised or versioned. When retrieved by SmartPlant Instrumentation, changes in the process data
appear in the To Do list. SmartPlant Foundation Process Domain provides a repository for this data, distinct from the Instrumentation Engineer’s
environment. It also provides the only way to make instrumentation process data available in the SmartPlant Foundation Data W arehouse, since
SmartPlant Instrumentation does not publish this data, but only a view file.
Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) in one engineering
discipline, and by deciding how and when to apply these changes in another discipline later in the work process.



PBS is required.



SmartPlant Electrical and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.

Sharing Data between PFDs and P&IDs

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Data Between PFDs and P&IDs

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process

Initial process and equipment data is created in Aspen Basic Engineering (ABE), then PFDs, data sheets (or lists) for equipment, streams, or instruments are
published to SmartPlant® Foundation. The PFDs, data sheets, or lists are retrieved into SmartPlant P&ID where the instruments, equipment, and nozzles are
placed, stream data is correlated to pipe runs, and then the pipe is routed on the P&ID. Once the P&ID is updated and published, ABE retrieves the P&ID
and runs their To Do List tasks.

Business Value



Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items) on the PFDs, and by
deciding how and when to apply these changes on the P&IDs

  Save man-hours by electronically transitioning information between disciplines

  Eliminate manual re-entry errors and improve communication between disciplines to reduce rework

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.





Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Data Between PFDs and P&IDs

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on the Intergraph Smart Support web site for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M
products used in your environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Data Between PFDs and P&IDs

3

Publishing process timing and frequency

Retrieving process timing and frequency

  Consideration must be made for the readiness of the PFDs and the
process data. If the process data is provided prematurely, process
design may be impacted.

  Publish each PFD or data sheet as soon as it contains design
information ready to form the basis for the process design.

  Additions, deletions, and major updates to equipment data should be

published as soon as they can be approved, because they are critical to
downstream disciplines.

Integration Capability Statements Sharing Data Between PFDs and P&IDs

  The project must plan for intervals where the P&IDs owned by a workshare
satellite are transferred to the host site in order to retrieve PFDs and data
sheets. Progress on the P&IDs should not continue at the Satellite (although
this is technically possible in standalone workshare) while the Host is retrieving
PFDs and data sheets, until all these P&IDs have been transferred back to the
Satellite. (11,22)

Aspen Basic Engineering

SmartPlant P&ID

Other tools that participate in this integration on the publish side

Other tools that participate in this integration on the retrieve side

  NA

  NA

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints
apply

  Aspen Basic Engineering (ABE) publishes streams and their process
data which are retrieved by SmartPlant P&ID. However, ABE will not
retrieve updates to process data published by SmartPlant P&ID.

  Published PFDs include all process stream data that would also be

shown on process data sheets.

  Data sheets are not limited equipment, but can include instruments and

also be lists of streams, equipment, and instruments.

  Equipment data sheets can include the related nozzles, if needed. If

related nozzles are included with equipment data sheets, these nozzles
are retrieved by SmartPlant P&ID and maintain the relationship to the
respective equipment.

  Plant item to PBS relationship is not retrieved by ABE. Therefore, if the
PBS relationship to equipment is expected in ABE, this is a manual
action to create relationship.

Work process aspects to consider and what limitations/constraints apply

  When a stream is first retrieved, it will result in a Create task for a pipe run.
When that task is executed, a small piece of pipe that contains all of the
stream data is placed inside the P&ID. At that point, the Correlate command
allows the user to correlate to other pipe runs that are part of the same stream
(The Correlate command copies all process data coming from the stream to
the pipe run(s) chosen.).

  When an updated stream is retrieved again, all pipe runs related to that stream

will receive Update tasks.



If a stream is deleted from a PFD, SmartPlant P&ID receives an update task to
uncorrelate and null the process data, but it does not delete the pipe run (s).

  When using SmartPlant applications in an integrated environment, editing

objects in a document previously published, such as a P&ID, requires work
processes that consider the impact of your edits on downstream disciplines.
For example, traditionally, an engineer marks up a P&ID to move part of the
items to another P&ID. The mark ups communicate a need to delete the items
from the source P&ID and create new items on the target P&ID. In an
integrated environment, when the P&ID is republished, other downstream
applications that have previously retrieved the P&ID receive Delete and Create
tasks during the next retrieve. These tasks can unnecessarily impact system
performance and create more work for the downstream disciplines to resolve
the resulting inconsistencies.

1.

2.

If items need to be moved within the same P&ID, instead of deleting and
re-creating them, simply move them within the drawing.

If items need to be moved from one P&ID to another P&ID, do not delete
them from the source P&ID. Instead, use SmartPlant P&ID’s Move to
Drawing command to move the items to the new P&ID. This command
preserves all data, including internal UIDs.

You should disconnect objects to be moved from existing objects or break the
piperuns so that the area can be easily selected with a fence select. Both
drawings will need to be republished.

Develop Installation Details – Detailed Design – Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Instrumentation

1

Develop Installation Details - Detailed Design - Level 2

1.  As an instrument engineer, I need to create hook up types so that I can categorize my installation details.
2.  As an instrument engineer, I need to create an item list library so that I can assign items to hook ups. This list may come from external sources, such as spreadsheets or other projects.
3.  As an instrument engineer, I need to create installation graphics or reuse graphics from external sources so that they can be assigned to hook up drawings.
4.  As an instrument engineer, I need to create the hook ups so that I can associate instrument tags to them.
5.  As an instrument engineer, I need to associate instrument tags to hook ups, using the instrument specifications as input, so that I can issue the installation details and create materials reports for Procurement.
6.  As a piping designer (or Smart3D administrator), I need to create or reuse 3D symbols to represent the correct installation of the instrument based on the hook ups.
7.  As a piping designer, I need to place (locate or relocate) instruments in the model and provide location information to the instrument engineer. The instrument is placed by selecting it from the P&ID in the P&ID Viewer window.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Using Instrument Dimensional Data to Drive 3D Design

8.  As a piping designer (or Smart3D administrator), I need to create drawings or reports with instrument location information so that the instrument engineer can verify that the installation details are correct.
9.  <decision point> As an instrument engineer, I need to verify that the instrument installation arrangement and location are both correct so that either the hook up or the instrument location can be changed, if necessary.
10.  <decision point> As an instrument engineer, I need to decide if the arrangement is valid so that the hook up can be changed, if necessary. If the arrangement is valid, then it is the location that is not valid, and I need to ask the piping designer to

relocate the instrument in the 3D model.

11.  As an instrument engineer, I need to create reports for the materials required for all the hook ups, so that the materials can be procured.
12.  As a member of the Procurement department, I need to import the bills of materials for the hook ups in SmartPlant Materials, so that the materials can be manages in SmartPlant Materials and procured.
13.  As a member of the Procurement department, I need to procure the materials for the hook ups so that the instruments can be hooked up in the field.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Instrumentation

2

Review of Intergraph Smart™ 3D Projects in SmartPlant Foundation

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Review of Intergraph Smart™ 3D Projects in SmartPlant Foundation

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions are
italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software, with
required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in the

Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process
This work process provides review of the Intergraph Smart™ 3D model (graphic and attribute data), and extended data from other integrated tools, in
SmartPlant Foundation. Within SmartPlant Foundation, you can review the model and data within the SmartPlant Foundation 3D view control, SmartPlant
Markup Plus, or SmartPlant Review.
The full capabilities of SmartPlant Review are available when SmartPlant Review is launched from SmartPlant Foundation. Comments created in SmartPlant
Review must be manually provided to the designers, or automatically sent using SmartPlant Foundation e-mail notifications, and reviewed for appropriate
actions in the respective applications.
Models opened and viewed with SmartPlant Markup Plus can be immediately commented upon by activating the appropriate toolbars in the view control.
SPRDirect is not intended for publishing to SmartPlant Foundation. To find out more about SPRDirect, see Review of Smart 3D Projects in SmartPlant Review
capability statement.

Business Value
When reviewing 3D models, SmartPlant Foundation provides a centralized location for targeted design reviews and walk-throughs for non-S3D users, with
annotation capabilities. SmartPlant Foundation allows you to find 3D objects based on their properties and view them in context with the plant. When viewing
3D objects, enterprise integration maintains relationships across all SmartPlant tools and documents stored in SmartPlant Foundation to fully review the design
and navigate the plant hierarchy.
Designers & Engineers
  Create better designs by navigating the 3D model and data on the fly within SmartPlant Review, while modeling in Smart 3D. (Requires dual monitors).
  Make better engineering design judgments by accessing the model data

Improve maintainability, safety, and accuracy through a variety of communication tools
  Promote constructability design with the ability to preview possible construction sequences
EPC Project Management
  Speed and improve decision-making process by accessing up-to-date information

  Ensure effective team communication between disciplines using the tagging features

Improve facilities management through direct access to documentation and maintenance records

Integration Capability Statements Review of Intergraph Smart™ 3D Projects in SmartPlant Foundation

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Improve safety by performing safety reviews with visualization

Construction
  Reduce costs by allocating more personnel resources to designing and less on reviewing
  Speed the schedule by defining the critical path and making it more complete

Plant Owners
  Enhance early problem detection with in-depth model review during detailed design
  Consider design alternatives much earlier in the design cycle by pinpointing potential problem areas

Improve facilities management through direct access to documentation and maintenance records

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.
  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the PBS
is required.
Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.


  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.
Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.
Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.



  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on eCustomer for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M products used in your environment,

as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant Foundation
database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.

Integration Capability Statements Review of Intergraph Smart™ 3D Projects in SmartPlant Foundation

3

Intergraph Proprietary Information









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.
In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs, should
consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.
If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed until
the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.
If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply publish

the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of

recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is alerted
that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user must

have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

Schema matches the codelist numerical value in the 3D Catalog Schema.

uses the automatic mapping between codelist value and SmartPlant
Schema Number by default. However, unlike the retrieve and
compare mapping, when necessary, you may establish a new
mapping by manually creating a publish relationship between a Smart
3D codelist entry and an enumerated list entry in the SmartPlant
schema.

General Considerations

What is the document container (e.g. P&ID) being published?    Smart 3D Model Graphics (.zvf) and Data (.xml)
For what other purposes can this published document be used?
  Viewing and navigation of the model in conjunction with documents and data from other disciplines
  Providing cable data to SmartPlant Electrical
  Consistency checking in SmartPlant Foundation
  Launching SmartPlant Review for detailed design reviews with data from SmartPlant Foundation
  Construction sequencing and model review with SmartPlant Construction
What is the scope of data/content being published OOTB, or where can I find that information in the user documentation?
SPEDataFlowDiagrams.pdf available on eCustomer and delivered with SmartPlant Foundation and the SmartPlant Adapter for Intergraph Smart™ 3D Guide.
Does this integration support publish/compare in SPF (e.g. is it a likely candidate for interdisciplinary consistency check)? Yes.
Is a graphic file published and is it hotspotted in SPF? Yes.
Are there limitations on auto-correlation in SPF? Only for uniquely tagged objects like equipment and inline instruments. Nozzles, pipe runs, offline
instruments, and ports are not handled.
What is the content being extracted out of the document container being retrieved?    All published data is extracted and loaded into a publish domain in
SmartPlant Foundation.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Instrumentation

1

Develop Instrument Index (Rev 0) - Basic Engineering - Level 2

1.  As an instrument engineer, I need to define instrument types and instrument type profiles for use by SmartPlant Instrumentation.
2.  As an instrument engineer, I need to define macro expansion types to handle the cases where I need to map one instrument shown on the P&ID to several instruments in SmartPlant Instrumentation so that all the required instruments can be created

in SmartPlant Instrumentation when retrieving the P&IDs.

3.  As a P&ID administrator, I need to create macro expansion types to match those defined in SmartPlant Instrumentation.
4.  As a member of the Process department, I need to develop the P&IDs so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.
5.  As a member of the Process department, I need to publish the P&IDs so that they are available for retrieval.
6.

If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant
Foundation.

7.  After documents with data are published and loaded in the SmartPlant Foundation data warehouse, it is possible to check the consistency of data coming from various tools for the objects published in that document, such as instruments, equipment,

Intergraph Proprietary Information

or pipe runs. See the applicable subprocess.

8.  As an instrument engineer, I need to retrieve the P&IDs so that I can create the instrument index.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Checking Instrument Consistency Between P&IDs and Instrumentation

9.  As a member of the Project Engineering department, I need to provide the updated equipment list to instrumentation so that it can be used to create the preliminary instrument index (Rev 0). Optionally, the equipment list can be created with

SmartPlant Foundation Authoring or with Aspen Basic Engineering.

10.  As an instrument engineer, I need to use the instrument index, the retrieved P&IDs, my previous project data, and/or the equipment list to create the preliminary instrument index (Rev 0).
11.  As an instrument engineer, I need to continue developing instrumentation so that I can specify the instruments required for the project.
12.  <decision point> As an instrument discipline lead, I need to decide whether it is time to share the instrument data with the Process discipline and the Electrical discipline, based on project guidelines and data readiness.
13.  As an instrument engineer, I need to publish the instrument index so that it is available for retrieval by other disciplines.
14.  If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant

Foundation.

15.  After documents with data are published and loaded in the SmartPlant Foundation data warehouse, it is possible to check the consistency of data coming from various tools for the objects published in that document, such as instruments, equipment,

or pipe runs. See the applicable subprocess.

16.  As a member of the Process department, I need to retrieve the instrument index so that I can update the P&IDs.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Checking Instrument Consistency Between P&IDs and Instrumentation

17.  As a member of the Process department, I need to develop P&IDs by creating, updating, and/or deleting instruments and loops on my P&IDs, based on the retrieved instrument index, so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other

disciplines.

18.  As a member of the Process department, I need to publish the P&IDs so that they are available for retrieval.
19.  If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant

Foundation.

20.  After documents with data are published and loaded in the SmartPlant Foundation data warehouse, it is possible to check the consistency of data coming from various tools for the objects published in that document, such as instruments, equipment,

or ripe runs. See the applicable subprocess.

21.  As an instrument engineer, I need to retrieve the P&IDs, so that I can update the instrument index.

See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:
  Checking Instrument Consistency Between P&IDs and Instrumentation

22.  As an instrument engineer, I need to continue developing instruments and loops so that I can specify the instruments required for the project and to update and share the instrument index (Rev 0).
23.  <decision point> As an instrument engineer, I need to determine if the instrument index (Rev 0) is ready to be issued or if further instrumentation work is needed.
24.  As a member of the Control Systems department, I need to develop the control system summary and I/O count so I can size the requirements and get all the quantities required.
25.  As an instrument engineer, I need to gather all the counts so that I can create the first check instrumentation cost estimate for Project Controls to create the first check project cost estimate.
26.  As a member of the Project Controls team, I need to create the first check project cost estimate using, among other things, the first check instrumentation cost estimate so that the information can be used in project decision-making.
27.  As an instrument engineer, I need to issue the instrument index (Rev 0).

Develop Instrument Specifications – Basic Engineering – Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Instrumentation

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Develop Instrument Specifications - Basic Engineering - Level 2

  This process is also used in the FEED stage for specifying long-lead instruments, at step 6 in the FEED Level 1 diagram.

1.  As an instrument engineer, I need to use the instrument index so that I can start specifying instrument data. This task can include the specifications form assignment and defining some information, such as line number and equipment number.
2.  As a process engineer, I need to provide process data through any of my preferred sources, such as spreadsheets, so that the instrument engineer can populate the instrument process data.
3.  As a process engineer, I need to provide stream process data through the P&ID pipe runs so that the instrument engineer can populate the instrument process data.
4.  As a member of the Process department, I need to publish the P&IDs so that the data can be made available to other disciplines.
5.  As a process engineer using SmartPlant Foundation Authoring, I need to provide hydraulic process data so that the instrument engineer can populate the instrument process data.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Creating Instrument Process Data Sheets in SmartPlant Foundation

6.  As a process engineer, I need to publish the process data sheet to make them available for retrieval.
7.

If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant
Foundation.

8.  As an instrument engineer, I need retrieve the process data sheet so that I can update the process data in SmartPlant Instrumentation.
9.

If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant
Foundation.

10.  As an instrument engineer, I need retrieve the P&IDs so that I can update the process data in SmartPlant Instrumentation.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Checking Instrument Consistency Between P&IDs and Instrumentation

11.  As an instrument engineer, I need to develop the instrument process data so that I can run calculations, if necessary, and update the specifications.
12.  After documents with data are published and loaded in the SmartPlant Foundation data warehouse, it is possible to check the consistency of data coming from various tools for the objects published in that document, such as instruments, equipment,

or pipe runs. See the applicable subprocess.

13.  As an instrument engineer using SmartPlant Foundation, I need to run the vessel nozzle summary report so that Process can develop the vessel analytical documentation.
14.  As a process engineer, I need to develop the vessel analytical view (documentation) of what the vessel should do so that the instrument engineers can create the vessel sketches. The vessel analytical documentation is also used in the bid process to

purchase the vessels.

15.  As an instrument engineer, I need to develop vessel sketches so that the Piping designers can determine nozzle and platform locations. These sketches are also used to define the calibration of the instrumentation.
16.  <decision point> As an instrument engineer, I need to decide, based on project guidelines, whether this instrument requires sizing calculations so that I can run the calculation through the SmartPlant Instrumentation calculation module.
17.  As an instrument engineer, I need to run preliminary calculations so that I can provide more data to further define instruments and to the vendor.
18.  As a member of the Safety team, I need to perform HAZOP and other safety studies to do hazardous and operation studies of the plant to show there are no hazards.
19.  As an instrument engineer, I need to update the instrument specification sheets, either from SmartPlant Instrumentation calculations or the vendor, so that I can further define instruments and issue to the vendor. I also need to verify that the vendor’s

data is correct.

20.  As a vendor using SmartPlant Instrumentation’s External Data Editor, I need to receive the updated specification sheets from the instrument engineer so that I can select the appropriate instruments and provide a quote.
21.  <decision point> As an instrument engineer, I need to check for and react to any changes in the process data so that I can update the instrument specification sheets, if necessary.

Enterprise Work Process for Instrumentation

2

Generate Isometric Drawings - Basic Engineering & Detailed Design - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

DSPE1-PE-200130A - Updated Enterprise Work Process for Piping and Equipment Design

1

Generate Isometric Drawings - Basic Engineering & Detailed Design - Level 2

1.  As a piping designer, I need to develop the 3D piping/equipment design so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines, continually reviewing the To Do list and IFC list to resolve inconsistencies and interferences.

2.  As a piping designer, I need to create and update isometric drawings, so that they can be used for fabrication and construction. The isometric drawings need to be checked for readability and completeness and undergo an inter-discipline review. This

review may result in updates to the model and a subsequent update of the isometric drawings before they are published.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Creating Isometrics with SmartPlant Isometrics for Documentation and Fabrication

3.  As a pipe fabricator, I need to generate spool drawings using SmartPlant SpoolGen based on the pcf file from the isometric drawing extraction, so that the spools can be fabricated. I will send the spool drawings back to the engineering company for

their review. Note that the piping designer can also create spool drawings using Smart 3D’s internal capability.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

4.  As a piping designer, I need to update the 3D model based on feedback from fabrication (spool drawings) and other design criteria. See the work process Update 3D Model for more information.

5.  As a piping designer, I need to create/update orthographic drawings so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

6.  As a piping designer, I need to publish the orthographic drawings so that they can be used for construction.

7.  As a piping designer, I need to publish the 3D model so that it can be used by construction along with the orthographic and isometric drawings.

8.  As a piping designer, I need to publish isometric drawings so that they can be used for construction.

9.  As a member of the Construction team, I need to publish the spool drawings so that they can be read by SmartPlant Construction.

10.  If the document has publishable data, the document and data are loaded into SmartPlant Foundation and are subsequently available for use by other applications. If the document does not have publishable data, it is available for viewing in SmartPlant

Foundation.

11.  As a member of the Construction team, I need to pull the information in the 3D model, the orthographic drawings, the spool drawings and the isometric drawings into the SmartPlant Construction environment so that I can build work packages. I need to

take note of the revision status of the documents (such as IFC – issued for construction) to determine if the documents are not yet final and should be used for planning only, or if they are ready for actual construction.

12.  As a member of the Construction team, I need to build work packages so that I can start planning construction. For more information, refer to Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning.

DSPE1-PE-200130A - Updated Enterprise Work Process for Piping and Equipment Design

Manage WBS and BOMs - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Manage WBS and BOMs - Level 2

1.  As a materials discipline lead, I need to define the BOM structure, views, and attributes.
2.  As a materials discipline lead, I need to define and maintain the loading templates.
3.  As a materials discipline lead, I need to set up the revision control criteria so that SmartPlant Materials will maintain BOM data for each revision imported.
4.  As a project control engineer or project planner, I need to create project control and planning break down structure to define the common structure of the asset and reconcile information across various tools and departments.
5.  As a materials discipline lead, I need to define or verify the work breakdown structure (WBS) per discipline so that BOM data can be allocated against the correct node.
6.  <decision point> Is BOM data to be entered manually?
7.  As a materials engineer, I need to define the BOM data manually using SmartPlant Materials to create data that cannot currently be imported from other tools. This event might occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the

development of the design, if necessary.

8.  As a materials engineer, I need to assign and verify the revision of the BOM data so that SmartPlant Materials can automatically compare all different revision statuses coming from any tool.
9.  As a schematics designer, I need to extract BOM data from SmartPlant Enterprise schematics tools. This event will occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.
10.  As a 3D designer, I need to extract BOM data from 3D tools (Intergraph Smart™ 3D and PDS). This event will occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Creating Isometrics with SmartPlant Isometrics for Documentation and Fabrication

11.  As a fabricator, I need to extract BOM data from SmartPlant Spoolgen. This event will occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

  Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

12.  As a designer, I might extract BOM data from any non-SmartPlant Enterprise design tool for any discipline. This event might occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.
13.  As a fabricator, I might extract BOM data from a non-SmartPlant Enterprise fabrication tool, such as Tekla. This event might occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.
14.  As a designer or materials engineer, I might develop BOM data in another application, such as a spreadsheet or text file tool. This event will occur on a regular basis during the length of the project reflecting the development of the design.
15.  As a materials engineer, I need to import the BOM data against a specified revision. Each revision might be used for engineering and requisition where specific revisions would be used for construction purposes. During import, SmartPlant Materials

automatically compares the different revisions of the BOM data.

16.  As a materials engineer, I need to verify the BOM data, adjusting it as needed so that the BOM data is complete, correct, and ready for use in downstream processes.
17.  As a materials engineer, I need to compare different revisions of the BOM data looking for changes so that the appropriate actions can be taken.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

P&ID Guided 3D Design and Consistency Checking

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

3.  The step Review and approve for publish is a timing decision made by the project, depending on many factors. Some of these factors are discussed in

the Publish and Retrieve Considerations section below.

Short Description of Work Process

This work process can be used in the two situations that typically occur during design:

1.

If the P&IDs are created early enough to guide the 3D design, create objects in the 3D model directly from the P&IDs.

2.

If the 3D objects are created in Smart 3D before or at the same time that the P&IDs are drawn and published, correlate and check consistency between
the existing 3D design and the P&IDs.

The P&IDs from SmartPlant P&ID are published and retrieved into Smart 3D. While viewing the P&IDs within the Smart 3D environment, the P&ID topology
and data are used to create the 3D design, or to check the existing 3D design against the P&IDs for consistency.

Business Value

  When P&ID information is available early in the design cycle, guide the 3D designer to follow the P&ID design and reduce data entry.

  Automate the traditional P&ID yellow-off comparison to the 3D design.



Improve project schedule and data quality by automatically identifying changes on the P&IDs (updates, additions, deletions, and renamed items), and by
deciding how and when to apply these changes in the 3D model.

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.

Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.


Integration Capability Statements P&ID Guided 3D Design and Consistency Checking

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information



Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on the Intergraph Smart Support web site for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M
products used in your environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

  SmartPlant Instrumentation publishes instrument dimensional information
that is used to automatically size the 3D instrument when placed in the

  For integration purposes, items in SmartPlant P&ID typicals and

SmartPlant P&ID assemblies must be correlated individually. They
behave like regular items, since they exist as separate items that are
each published to Smart 3D.





In Smart 3D, P&ID typicals are individual items on a separate layer
that can be displayed On or Off.

In SmartPlant P&ID, an assembly is a collection of individual items
stored in the catalog as a single symbol object. When placed into a
P&ID, an assembly is broken into individual components that are
published to Smart 3D

On the other hand, a Macrocomponent is an object in the P&ID that
represents, or is made to look like, multiple items (for example, a nipple,
a valve and a plug), but in fact, is a single object when placed in
SmartPlant P&ID. This object may be defined in SmartPlant P&ID as a
valve (one item) in which case it would publish as a valve (one item).
When correlating, the Smart 3D user ignores the 3D nipple and 3D plug.

  The pipe run in Smart 3D must be correlated with the pipe run in

SmartPlant P&ID before correlating the 3D valve with the matching
macrocomponent in SmartPlant P&ID.

Macrocomponents can also include implied items, such as a valve cover,
not displayed on the P&ID. Implied items are not known to Smart 3D;
thus, do not require correlation.

Retrieving process timing and frequency

  The P&ID group should collaborate with the 3D group to communicate as
more and more of the details of the P&ID become ready to form the basis
of 3D layout.

  Time to execute a retrieve is directly proportional to the number of P&IDs
being retrieved and must be understood and planned accordingly. The
P&IDs are available for retrieval only after being published. (12)

  Additions, deletions, and major updates to equipment data should be

published as soon as they can be approved, because they are critical to

  Minimize time delay between P&ID publish and retrieval. The project
must plan for intervals in order to facilitate efficient and up-to-date

been published previously should result in the republishing of the

  Time to execute a background publish is directly proportional to the

number of P&IDs being published and must be understood and planned
accordingly. The P&IDs are available for retrieval only after being
published. (6)

  Minimize time delay between P&ID readiness and publish. The project
must plan for intervals in order to facilitate efficient 3D design. (6)

  The project must plan for intervals when all the P&IDs owned by a

workshare Satellite are transferred to the Host site in order to publish the
P&IDs. Progress on the P&IDs should not continue at the Satellite
(although this is technically possible in standalone workshare) until the
P&IDs have been transferred back to the Satellite. (5, 8)

  The 3D designer typically models from the P&IDs by viewing one P&ID in

6

SmartPlant P&ID

downstream disciplines. For example, traditionally, an engineer marks up
a P&ID to move part of the items to another P&ID. The mark ups
communicate a need to delete the items from the source P&ID and
create new items on the target P&ID. In an integrated environment, when
the P&ID is republished, other downstream applications that have
previously retrieved the P&ID receive Delete and Create tasks during the
next retrieve. These tasks can unnecessarily impact system performance
and create more work for the downstream disciplines to resolve the
resulting inconsistencies.

1.

2.

If items need to be moved within the same P&ID, instead of deleting
and re-creating them, simply move them within the drawing.

If items need to be moved from one P&ID to another P&ID, do not
delete them from the source P&ID. Instead, use SmartPlant P&ID’s
Move to Drawing command to move the items to the new P&ID.
This command preserves all data, including internal UIDs.

  You should disconnect objects to be moved from existing objects or

break the piperuns so that the area can be easily selected with a fence

actuator are published by SmartPlant P&ID as two separate objects.
For Smart 3D, the component (symbol) includes both combined together
as one. In this situation, the user will correlate to the instrument on the
P&ID and ignore the associated actuator.

  When using integration, only embedded P&ID border files are supported.

Do not use linked border files.

  SmartPlant P&ID drawings can now include ducting (HVAC) objects,
giving users the ability to create D&IDs. Although the drawings
containing ducting objects can be published to SmartPlant Foundation
and retrieved into Smart 3D, the ducting objects themselves are not yet

been viewed. (15)

  For important precautions regarding the handling of 3D pipelines, and

considerations when retrieving or not retrieving the PBS into Smart 3D,
please see the table in the capability statement Defining a PBS for
Integration. (18)

  An attribute break or an Off Page Connector (OPC) on the P&ID requires
a break in the 3D model for correlation of runs.    The best case scenario
is to make this a natural break, for instance, a weld between a pipe and
an elbow or an existing flange.    (19)

  Correlation can be performed manually or automatically. Manual

correlation is performed using the Correlate with Design Basis command,
and automatic correlation is performed with the Correlate Automatically
command.    (18)

  The system allows a one-to-one or one (P&ID) to many (3D) correlation

of runs or pipelines. However, many (P&ID) to one (3D) or many-to-many
correlations are not yet possible. This must be considered for handling
instances when the schematic representation is different from the 3D
model which occurs for legitimate reasons. (18)



In a one-to-many case, automatic correlation only correlates one of the
many 3D runs that are to be correlated with the P&ID run. (18)

  Correlation of existing 3D runs with corresponding "zero-length" runs in
the P&ID is improved using automatic correlation in Smart 3D. (18)

  You can approve differences between the P&ID and the 3D pipelines

and piperuns by setting their status to Topology mismatch approved or
Data and topology mismatch approved. However, when you publish
the 3D model, approved data differences can still appear as
inconsistencies in SmartPlant Foundation. (19)

  Some objects are shown with a single symbol on the P&ID but are fully

modeled with all their individual parts in the 3D model ( e.g. vents, drains,
pressure indicators, etc.) resulting in the 3D objects which are not
represented on the P&ID being ignored for correlation purposes.

Also, some items represented on the P&ID will not appear at all in the 3D
model; e.g. control system functions and signal lines.

Both of these types of exceptions can be configured to be ignored for
correlation using the file ExemptCorrelateClasses.xml in the Smart 3D

Mapping considerations for publish

Mapping considerations for retrieve

  As delivered, not all temperatures and pressures are mapped. The

  Ensure that the Number for the enumerated list entry in the SmartPlant

following are mapped between SmartPlant P&ID and Smart 3D:    Design
Min Temp, Design Min Pressure, Design Max Temp, Design Max
Pressure, Oper Min Temp, Oper Min Pressure, Oper Max Temp and
Oper Max Pressure.

  SmartPlant P&ID 2009 Service Pack 4 and 2009 Service Pack 3 HF10
introduced the relationship OwnsComponentOcc (OCC) to correctly
assign component ownership at specification breaks. By default, this
relationship is not mapped between the SmartPlant P&ID map file
(SPPIDDataMap.xml) and the SmartPlant Schema.

  When publishing P&ID drawings for use by Smart 3D, you should
update the SPPIDDataMap.xml to include the OCC relationship. If
you do not include this relationship, the new auto-correlation in
Smart 3D 2011 R1 and later places the piping component in the
ambiguous section of the auto-correlate dialog instead of assigning
the component to the correct piperun.

  However, when publishing P&ID drawings for use by SmartPlant

Instrumentation 2009 Service Pack 2 or earlier, you should continue
to exclude this relationship. The relationship may cause SmartPlant
Instrumentation to switch between the two piperuns at the spec
break each time the P&ID is retrieved.

Schema matches the codelist numerical value in the 3D Catalog
Schema. When these values match, list values for mapped properties
that use the lists are automatically mapped based on those numbers.

  The retrieve mapping is created when you generate the design basis

and is not editable using the Schema Editor. Therefore, the codelist
numerical value and the SmartPlant Schema Number must match for
the value to be retrieved.

  Smart 3D uses DesignBasis_map.xml mapping when data is

retrieved. Comparison mapping is stored in the
SP3DPublishMap.xml, and while it may be edited in the Schema
Editor, it requires the codelist numerical value and SmartPlant
Schema Number to match in order to allow the property values to be
correlated in the 3D model.

  Smart 3D publish mapping, also stored in the SP3DPulbishMap.xml,
uses the automatic mapping between codelist value and SmartPlant
Schema Number by default. However, unlike the retrieve and
compare mapping, when necessary, you may establish a new
mapping by manually creating a publish relationship between a
Smart 3D codelist entry and an enumerated list entry in the
SmartPlant schema.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

General Considerations

  What is the document container (eg P&ID) being published?    P&ID.

  For what other purposes can this published document be used?    The P&ID graphics and data are available in SmartPlant Foundation for viewing

and navigation by project personnel.    In addition, the same P&ID documents that are used for this workflow can be used in integration workflows
involving SmartPlant Instrumentation and SmartPlant Electrical.

  What is the scope of data/content being published OOTB, or where can I find that information in the user documentation?

SPEDataFlowDiagrams.pdf available on the Intergraph Smart Support web site and delivered with SmartPlant Foundation, and the SmartPlant Adapter
for SmartPlant P&ID Guide, SmartPlant Adapter for Intergraph Smart™ 3D Guide.

  Can the published data be automatically correlated in SPF to facilitate running interdisciplinary inconsistency reports? Consistency checking

reports can be run in SmartPlant Foundation on the correlated items per the restrictions on correlation described below.



Is a graphic file published and is it hotspotted in SPF?    Yes.

  Are there limitations on auto-correlation in SPF?    Correlation that takes place in SmartPlant Foundation as a result of publishing data is performed
based on matching item tags.    Therefore, items in the P&ID and 3D model that are uniquely tagged, such as equipment and inline instruments can be
correlated. Nozzles, pipe runs, offline instruments and ports are not handled. Automated correlation based on topology is not supported.

  What is the content being extracted out of the document container being retrieved?

  Significant data considered by 3D:

  Topology

  Short Code (piping components) and ItemTag (instruments and specialty piping components) and Classification (equipment)

  Process data
