Purchase Materials - Level 1 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

1

Purchase Materials - Level 1

1.  As a procurement manager, I need to manage the supplier and contractor information so that our company will have up-to-date information on the suppliers and contractors to support sourcing our material requirement and work.

See the work process Manage Supplier and Contractor Information - Level 2 Diagram (on page 3) for more information.

2.  As a discipline lead, I need to release the requisition to procurement so that the purchasing process can begin.

3.  <decision point> As a project procurement manager or coordinator, I need to decide if the requisition should use an inquiry process which would be based on the project procurement strategy or execution plan.

4.  As a buyer, I need to create or revise the inquiry for the material in the requisition. This process will include selecting bidders, adding information such as bidding instructions, terms and conditions, shipping instructions, and so forth, and issuing the

Intergraph Proprietary Information

inquiry.

5.  As a project stakeholder, I need to approve the inquiry.

6.  As a supplier, I might issue a bid (offer a quote, offer a tender, etc.).

7.  As a discipline lead (requisition owner), I need to perform a technical evaluation of the bid.

8.  As a buyer, I need to finalize the commercial evaluation of the bids so that the optimum selection is made to support the overall project objectives.

9.  As a project stakeholder, I need to approve the bid tabulation.

See the work process Manage Inquiries - Level 2 Diagram (on page 5) for more information.

10.  As a buyer, I need to create and maintain an agreement, a purchase order requisition, a blanket agreement, or a letter of intent, and so forth.

See the work process Create and Maintain Agreement - Level 2 Diagram (on page 7) for more information.

11.  As a project stakeholder, I need to approve and issue the agreement.

12.  As a project planner, I need to manage and report on the progress and status of the materials and services.

See the work process Manage Progress and Status - Level 2 Diagram (on page Error! Bookmark not defined.) for more information.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

2

Manage Supplier and Contractor Information - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

3

Manage Supplier and Contractor Information - Level 2

The following diagram illustrates receiving and maintaining company information for suppliers and contractors using the SmartPlant Materials Portal. However, the same process might be performed by manually entering the data directly into the
SmartPlant Materials database using a different format for gathering the data.

1.  As a SmartPlant Materials administrator, I need to set up the SmartPlant Materials Portal for suppliers and contractors.

2.  As a procurement manager, I need to set up the company profile and qualifications questionnaire for potential/prospective suppliers and contractors.

3.  As a supplier or contractor, I need to request access to the SmartPlant Materials Portal, providing basic company information for consideration.

4.  As a member of the procurement team, I need to verify basic company information for suppliers and contractors requesting access to the SmartPlant Materials Portal. If validated, I need to grant the company access so that they can provide further

Intergraph Proprietary Information

information.

5.  As a supplier or contractor, I need to submit answers to the company profile questionnaire via the SmartPlant Materials Portal.

6.  As a member of the procurement team, I need to review the submitted company profiles.

7.  <decision point> As a member of the procurement team, I need to approve or reject the company profile. Rejected companies are notified via email and will not be considered further.

8.  As a supplier or contractor, I need to submit the company qualifications and material/work group information.

9.  As a member of the procurement team, I need to review the supplier or contractor for inquiry only, or for contract, for a specific project or for all projects.

10.  <decision point> As a member of the procurement team, I need to approve or reject the supplier or contractor's qualifications for scope.

11.  As a supplier or contractor, I will update my company information, as necessary or requested.

12.  As a member of the project team, I might rate the supplier’s or contractor’s performance in the bidding and delivery process.

13.  As a member of the procurement team, I need to maintain supplier information.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

4

Manage Inquiries - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

5

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Manage Inquiries - Level 2

1.  As a project procurement manager, I need to acknowledge the requisition and assign a buyer who will execute the inquiry process.

2.  As a buyer, I need to create or revise the inquiry (RFQ).

3.  As a SmartPlant Foundation user, I need to manage the engineering and supporting documents for the project so that technical information will be available downstream.

4.  As a document controller, I need to manage engineering and supporting documents for the project so that technical information will be available downstream.

5.  As a buyer, I need to attach the supporting documents to the inquiry.

6.  As a buyer, I need to maintain the inquiry by defining bidders, bidding instructions, exchange rates, confirmation of quotes, evaluation checklist, and so forth.

7.  As a buyer/project procurement manager, I need to approve and issue the inquiry to bidders. Depending on the company and the project requirements, the inquiry may be subject to an extensive approval process.

8.  As a supplier, I need to prepare and finalize the bid, including both commercial and technical information.

9.  <decision point> As a buyer/project procurement manager, I need to determine if the bid is complete and commercially acceptable.

10.  <decision point> As a requisition owner, I need to decide if a technical evaluation is required.

11.  As a requisition owner, I need to perform a technical evaluation of the bid to ensure the requisition specifications are met.

12.  As a supplier, I might initiate a request for information process to clarify technical or commercial issues with the buyer or the requisition owner. As a buyer, I might also initiate a request for information process to the bidder. This will be a continuous

discussion between the supplier and buyer.

13.  As a buyer, I need to finalize the commercial evaluation of the bids.

14.  As a buyer, I need to finalize the bid tabulation.

15.  As a project stakeholder, I need to approve the selections as proposed by the buyer and resulting from the bid tabulations.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

6

Create and Maintain Agreement - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

7

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Create and Maintain Agreement - Level 2

1.  As a buyer, I need to identify the agreement type and the source of the available data that will be part of the agreement.

2.

(Option A) As a buyer, I might define the source as a manual agreement.

3.

(Option B) As a buyer, I might define the source as a quote summary.

4.

(Option C) As a buyer, I might define the source as a blanket order.

5.

(Option D) As a buyer, I might define the source as an engineering requisition.

6.

(Option E) As a buyer, I might define the source as a corporate/free issue material warehouse.

7.  As a buyer, I need to create or revise the agreement. Based on the selected source, I will do one of the following things.

  For option A, I will select a supplier and enter all information about agreement line items, prices, terms and conditions.

  For option B, I will select one approved quote summary from those defined in the system so that available agreement information will be populated automatically.

  For option C, I will select the blanket order and appropriate requisition, to which I will add quantities, delivery dates, delivery places, and possibly required attachments.

  For option D, I will select one approved engineering requisition for purchase from those defined in the system so that available agreement information will be populated automatically. To that I will add a supplier, prices, terms and conditions,

delivery dates, delivery places, incoterms, and possibly required attachments.

  For option E, I will select the items from the corporate warehouse or free issue warehouse of the owner so that the material will be available onsite. I might add an account code, delivery dates, and delivery places.

8.  As a SmartPlant Foundation user, I need to manage the engineering and supporting documents for the agreement so that technical, legal, and commercial information will be available.

9.  As a document controller, I need to manage engineering and supporting documents for the agreement so that technical, legal, and commercial information will be available.

10.  As a buyer, I need to attach the supporting documents to the agreement.

11.  As a project control manager, I need to define the account structure.

12.  As a buyer, I need to maintain the agreement as defined in step 7.

13.  As a buyer, I need to validate the agreement and submit it for approval by the project team.

14.  As a project team, we will approve and issue the agreement.

15.  As a supplier, I need to accept and acknowledge the agreement.

16.  As a finance manager, I need to register the financial commitment. This process is typically automated through customization creating an interface with the finance system of the user.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

8




# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\MTR.pdf
---

Tag Register Production - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Tag Register Production - Level 2

1.  As a member of the Process department, I need to develop P&IDs so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines and update the line list.

2.  As a member of the Process department, I need to publish the P&IDs so that the information is available to other disciplines.

3.  As an instrument engineer, I need to continue developing instrumentation so that I can specify the instruments required for the project.

4.  As an instrument engineer, I need to publish the instrument index so that it is available for retrieval by other disciplines.

5.  As an electrical engineer, I need to develop the electrical design so that the information is available to other disciplines.

6.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to publish the cable schedule.

7.  As a member of the electrical department, I need to publish the electrical equipment.

8.  As an information manager, I need to create tag register documents using tag register definitions so that tags and their technical data can be accumulated for handover to the owner.

9.  As a piping designer, I need to develop the 3D model so that I can convey the updated status of the plant to other disciplines.

10.  As a piping designer, I need to publish the 3D model so that so that the information is available to other disciplines.

11.  As a piping designer, I need to publish isometric drawings so that the information is available to other disciplines.

12.  As a piping designer, I need to publish isometric drawings so that the information is available to other disciplines.

13.  As a piping designer or member of the Construction team, I need to publish the spool drawings so that the information is available to other disciplines.

14.  As a process engineer using SmartPlant Foundation Authoring, I need to develop the line list so that I can convey detailed pipe and fluid information not shown on the P&ID.

15.  As a SmartPlant Foundation user, I need to publish the line list so that the information can be shared with other disciplines.

16.  As a mechanical engineer using a custom SmartPlant Foundation Authored Equipment application, I need to develop the equipment data sheets.

17.  As a mechanical engineer, I need to publish the equipment data sheets so that the information is available to other disciplines.

18.  As an information manager, I need to receive documents from the vendors on my project so that their tags and technical data can be accumulated for handover to the owner.    This arrives in various formats (e.g. Excel, ASCII, PDF, Word, and so

forth).

19.  As an engineer or designer, I need to develop some other internal design data using non-SmartPlant applications as stipulated by my company’s standard operating procedures.

20.  As an information manager, I need to create and import Excel files containing data from internal non-SmartPlant applications into the Master Tag Registry so that this data can be accumulated for handover to the owner.

21.  The documents published by the SmartPlant applications or imported as Excel files are automatically loaded and consolidated into the Master Tag Registry.

22.  As an information manager, I need to review the tag data in the Master Tag Registry for readiness to generate the registers. I need to look for completeness of the technical data.

23.  As an information manager, I need to run inconsistency reports that check the data for the tag registers.

24.  As an information manager, I need to evaluate the inconsistency reports to see if any inconsistencies exist or any data is missing. If so, I need to communicate the inconsistencies to the responsible parties and determine if the inconsistencies are

acceptable in order to proceed with generating the tag registers. If the inconsistencies are not acceptable I will need to wait until they are resolved in step 25 before proceeding to step 26.

25.  As an engineer, designer or vendor, I need to change my design in order to be consistent with the other disciplines on the project. After resolving the inconsistencies, I need to publish, load or import the updated documents.

26.  As an information manager, I need to set a new revision, if needed, on the tag register documents.

27.  As an information manager, I need to generate the tag registers so that I can provide them to the owner.

28.  As an information manager, I need to transmit the tag registers to the owner.

Enterprise Work Process for the Master Tag Registry

2

CAESAR II® NUCLEAR
Pipe Stress Analysis for the Nuclear Industry

CAESAR II – World Leader in Pipe Stress Analysis
CAESAR II® is the world’s most respected tool for pipe stress analysis. Used by more

O/Os and EPCs, it is the solution against which all others are measured. CAESAR II

offers more than 34 international piping codes and is known for its technical superiority,

flexibility, ease of use, innovation, and reliability. CAESAR II now complies with ASME

NQA-1 2008/09 quality assurance (QA).

CAESAR II Nuclear – Building on a Proven Track Record
CAESAR II Nuclear is a unique solution that leverages the power and quality that

CAESAR II has built on for nearly 30 years. CAESAR II Nuclear complies with ASME

NQA-1 2008/09 QA standards required for nuclear facility design as endorsed by the

Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC). This new software solution boasts additional

• International piping codes.

features over CAESAR II, allowing for direct adoption in the nuclear sector for both new

• Extensive material databases.

and existing facilities.

• Steel databases and modeling.

• Expansion joint databases.

• Hanger databases.

• Hanger design.

• Automated stress iso creation.

• Links to Intergraph CADWorx Plant.

• Links to Intergraph Smart™ 3D.

ASME Nuclear Quality and Assurance
To ensure the total quality and integrity of CAESAR II Nuclear as required by the ASME

NQA-1, Intergraph® conducted hundreds of automated numerical, usability, and work-

flow tests. During development, all steps have test plans which incorporate multiple

review levels to ensure a robust and high-quality industry solution.

Commercial Grade Dedication
The Commercial Grade Dedication (CGD) acceptance process, as outlined in ASME

NQA-1-2012 Subpart 3.2-214, includes detailed verification of results using alternate

calculation tools and verification tests such as the NRC benchmarks. These results and

comparisons are published in a CGD Guide to document the software’s accuracy. The

CGD technical verification guide is also separately available for CAESAR II Nuclear users.

Quality Assurance and Reporting
CAESAR II Nuclear users in the U.S. can access QA reporting to keep them fully

informed about issues and software changes helping them comply with U.S. federal

requirements 10 CFR Part 50 App. B. and 10 CFR Part 21.

Quality You Can Trust
Following higher QA standards has improved the overall quality of the CAESAR II prod-

ucts as a whole. Both CAESAR II and CAESAR II Nuclear users can enjoy increased

• The solution offers a design and input verification tool, CAESAR II
QATest, for comparing benchmark output with the output gener-
ated for an installation. This enables users to satisfy their installa-
tion requirements.

• Enhanced, critical-level technical support is included for

nuclear licensees.

Technical Specifications
• Microsoft® Windows®-compatible.

confidence in a tool that has delivered reliable results for

three decades.

Links to Design
CAESAR II solutions offer many time-saving interfaces, such as

bi-directional links between plant design and engineering analy-

sis. These links between Intergraph Smart 3D and Intergraph

CADWorx® Plant Professional enable the transfer of design and

analysis data between design and analysis without data loss.

Links to Third-party Analysis Solutions
CAESAR II also connects to third-party analysis tools, which helps

leverage specialized analysis results to and from CAESAR II. These

links range from direct interface links to finite element and hydraulic

analysis, to links to other systems through the CAESAR II published

neutral file or Intergraph PCF file format.

Capabilities and Standards
CAESAR II Nuclear addresses the needs of the nuclear industry by

adhering to various nuclear standards and procedures. This helps

ensure that the program delivers the required quality this challenging

sector demands.

• The software is commercially dedicated using the new ASME
NQA-1-2012 Subpart 3.2-214 Commercial Grade Dedication
(CGD) acceptance process for software. This assures the product
has passed critical acceptance tests, analyses, and inspections,
as well as performance requirements, characteristics, and vendor
history.

• Support is offered for U.S. NRC requirements, 10 CFR Part 50

App. B (Quality Assurance Criteria for Nuclear Power Plants and
Fuel Reprocessing Plants) and 10 CFR Part 21 (Reporting of De-
fects and Noncompliance).

• For U.S. customers, a Quality Assurance and Reporting notifica-

tion service is included.

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\P&ID Engineering Integrity.pdf

P&ID Engineering Integrity Work Process – Level 1

P&ID Engineering Integrity Work Process – User Stories
1.  As a Standards Organization, we define and provide engineering best practices and standards, sometimes by industry, so that facilities are designed with

sound engineering practices for safe and efficient operation.

2.  As an Engineering company, we define our EPC corporate design standards to be used in our projects, based on experience and best practices, so that they

can be executed as efficiently and safely as possible.

3.  As an Engineering company, we create corporate standards based on best practices and industry standards, so that they can be used in projects.
4.  As an Owner company, we define our Owner design standards to be used during the design and operation of our facilities.
5.  As an Engineering company, we need to select the relevant design standards from our corporate library and create new ones as specified by the owner, to

be used on this particular project.

6.  As an Engineering company, we need to develop P&IDs for the project.
7.  As a process engineer, I need to check the P&IDs against the project standards, so that they comply with them.
8.  <Decision point> As a process engineer, I need to determine if the P&IDs comply with the project standards.
9.  <Decision point> As a process engineer, I need to determine if the non-compliance is acceptable.
10. As a process engineer, I need to notify the P&ID designer to correct the P&ID, so that it complies with the standards.
11. As a process engineer, I need to issue the P&IDs to the Owner, so that they can be reviewed.
12. As an Owner, I need to check the P&IDs against our design standards, so that they comply with them.
13. <Decision point> As an Owner engineer, I need to determine if the P&IDs comply with our design standards.
14. As an Owner company, we approve the P&IDs.

Validate P&ID Integrity Work Process – Level 2

Validate P&ID Integrity Work Process – User Stories
1.  As a Standards Organization, we define and provide engineering best practices and standards, sometimes by industry, so that facilities are designed with

sound engineering practices for safe and efficient operation.

2.  As an Engineering company, we define our EPC corporate design standards to be used in our projects, based on experience and best practices, so that they

can be executed as efficiently and safely as possible.

3.  As an administrator of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to create the rules based on the corporate and industry standards, so that they can

be used in projects.

4.  As an Owner company, we define our Owner design standards to be used during the design and operation of our facilities.
5.  As an administrator of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the Owner, I need to create the rules based on the corporate and industry standards, so that they

can be used in projects.

6.  As an administrator of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the Owner, I need to select the rules that apply to this particular project.
7.  As an administrator of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to select from our corporate library of rules for use in the project, and also import the

rules supplied by the Owner, so that they can be used on this particular project.

8.  As a SPP&ID user for the Engineering company, I need to develop P&IDs for the project.
9.  As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to identify the subset of rules that need to be used based on the current design phase of the

project, so that the P&IDs can be checked against them.

10. As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the Owner, I need to identify the subset of rules that need to be used based on the current design phase of the

project, so that the P&IDs can be checked against them.

11. As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to import the P&IDs into my environment, so that I can work on them.
12. As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to run the rules against a set of P&IDs so that the design complies with the standards.
13. <Decision point> As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the EPC, I need to determine if the P&IDs comply with the rules.
14. <Decision point> As a process engineer, I need to determine if the non-compliance is acceptable.
15. As a user of SPP&ID, I need to correct the P&ID, so that it complies with the standards.
16. As a process engineer, I need to issue the P&IDs to the Owner, so that they can be reviewed.
17. As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the Owner, I need to run the rules against a set of P&IDs received from the Engineering company so that the

design complies with the standards.

18. <Decision point> As a user of SPP&ID Engineering Integrity for the Owner, I need to determine if the P&IDs comply with the rules.
19. As an Owner company, we approve the P&IDs.

Interference Checking Detection (IFC): Overview

Smart 3D provides two methods for IFC operation:

Server-based Interference checking (database detection).

- Runs on a separate server as a Windows NT Service

- Looks for all interferences in the plant

Interactive interference checking (local detection).

- Helps the designer in real time

- Results are scoped to objects in a session (what you see in your
workspace)

Copyright © 1999-2012 Intergraph Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

Database Detect

Local Detect

Runs continuously (System Administrator choice)  Works only within the current session

Dedicated server minimizes impact on users and
improves performance

Provides immediate graphical feedback (works
immediately after commit)

Creates persistent interferences that are stored in
the model database

Creates temporary interference markers in a user
session

Based on administrator settings (controlled by
permission groups)

Based on individual user settings

Provides feed back on how much has been
checked

Checks only created and modified items

appear as a sphere

data.

displayed in this aspect will not be

Model Data Reuse:  Introduction

 Model Data Reuse (MDR) is a way to copy large amounts of 3D model

data in a robust, scalable and intelligent way

 MDR supplements the current copy/paste process – the existing

process remains in place for smaller sets of data

      MDR Results:

Copyright © 1999-2011 Intergraph Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

Model Data Reuse: Advantages

 MDR runs from Project Management environment as a wizard

– Interaction with objects is not required

 Does not require objects to be loaded into memory

– process is scalable and can copy much larger sets of data

 Software breaks down copy set into multiple transactions

– Process is robust and can tolerate a failure; it will continue to copy

the rest of the remaining objects

Copyright © 1999-2011 Intergraph Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

Model Data Reuse: Advantages

 Software determines the sequence in which to copy objects

 Permission group mapping to existing or default permission group

 Transformation can be chosen using coordinate systems

 Very good logging of process (including failures)

 Copy of model objects between plants is supported

Copyright © 1999-2011 Intergraph Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

 MDR command exists only in Project Management

1.  As a mechanical engineer, I need to update the equipment data sheets so that I can provide the project information to develop P&IDs.
2.  As a solution vendor (package, skid, or technology), I need to provide P&IDs so that they can be included in the project P&IDs.

  See the following Integration Capability Statement for more information:

Control Site - Level 1 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

1

Control Site - Level 1

1.  As a construction planner, I need to define the material work packages and their priorities.
2.  As a member of the material controls team, I will provide the status of materials.
3.  As a construction planner, I need to simulate materials availability for allocation, adjusting the priorities and the material as necessary and interacting with the material controls team to expedite material availability.
4.  As a construction planner, I need to allocate the required materials.

See the work process Allocate Materials - Level 2 Diagram (on page 3) for more information.

5.  As a material receiver, I need to receive the materials. If the materials have not been received in good order, I will initiate an over/short/damage process.
6.  As a warehouse manager, I manage the inventory of the stored materials and am responsible for all transactions related to materials in the warehouse.
7.  As a warehouse manager, I need to transfer and issue the required materials.
8.  As a member of the subcontractor/crew, I will receive the material and fabricate and/or install materials.

See the work process Manage, Fabricate, and Install Materials - Level 2 Diagram (on page 5) for more information.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

2

Allocate Materials - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

3

Allocate Materials - Level 2

1.  As a construction manager, I need to set up allocation information. This includes defining the sub-contractors, the materials work packages, and potential materials substitutions and transformations.
2.  As a construction planner, I need to define the materials work package strategy.
3.  As a construction supervisor, I need to create a construction work package that is consistent with the construction master scheduled and accommodates crew availability.

For more information, refer to the "Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning" document.

4.  As a construction supervisor, I need to create materials work packages that are consistent with the scope of what I will fabricate, install, and erect.
5.  As a construction supervisor, I need to select the warehouse in which the materials are located.
6.  As a construction supervisor, I need to define the priorities of the materials work package based on the master schedule.
7.  As a construction supervisor, I need to assign the priorities and constraints that would impact the work package while executing forecast.
8.  As a construction supervisor, I need to run the materials forecast.
9.  <decision point> As a construction supervisor, I need to determine if the materials forecast is satisfactory.
10.  As a construction supervisor, if the forecasts were not satisfactory, I need to request a resolution to the issues by the project team.
11.  As a construction supervisor, I need to report the materials status of the materials work packages that are part of the forecast scope.
12.  As a construction supervisor, I need to analyze the results of the forecast.
13.  As a construction supervisor, I need to reserve the allocated materials.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

4

Manage, Fabricate, and Install Materials - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

5

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Manage, Fabricate, and Install Materials - Level 2

(Option A) As a material receiver, I will select an agreement and relevant line items and quantity to receive. If the quantity received does not match the quantity expected, I will create an Over/Short/Damage report.
(Option B) As a material receiver, I will select a release note and relevant line items to receive. If the quantity received does not match the quantity expected, I will create an Over/Short/Damage report.
(Option C) As a material receiver, I will select a package to be received. If the quantity received does not match the quantity expected, I will create an Over/Short/Damage report.
(Option D) As a material receiver, I will receive materials that are not associated with an agreement. If the quantity received does not match the quantity expected, I will create an Over/Short/Damage report.

1.  As a construction manager, I need to define the structure of the site, including location and warehouses, roles for accessing the system, and rules, such as numbering convention, receiving status, and so forth.
2.  As a material receiver, I need to identify the source of the information on which the material receipt will be based.
3.
4.
5.
6.
7.  As an inspector, I will perform quality assurance/quality control inspection on the delivered materials.
8.  <decision point> As an inspector, I will determine if the materials meet the quality assurance/quality control requirements.
9.  As a material receiver, I need to create an Over/Short/Damage report for material that does not meet the quality assurance/quality control requirements.
10.  As a material receiver, I need to put into quarantine any materials that did not pass quality assurance/quality control inspection.
11.  As a warehouse manager, I need to store the materials in the warehouse.
12.  As a finance manager, I will receive notification that the materials were received. This process is typically automated through customization creating an interface with the finance system of the customer.
13.  As a warehouse manager, I might transfer materials to another storage facility. (This is an optional step. Not all materials will need to be transferred.)
14.  As a contractor supervisor/crew supervisor, I need to receive the material that I am going to use for fabrication or installation.
15.  As a warehouse manager, I might audit the warehouse to confirm the existence of the expected materials. (This is an optional step. Not all materials will be audited.)
16.  As a warehouse manager, I might reconcile properties and quantities of the materials on-hand. This might imply revising the material receiving report or the materials transfer report generated automatically by the system.
17.  (Option A) As a member of the warehouse crew, I might issue the required materials by BOM reservation.
18.  (Option B) As a member of the warehouse crew, I might issue the required materials by BOM scope.
19.  (Option C) As a member of the warehouse crew, I might issue the required materials by BOM item.
20.  (Option D) As a member of the warehouse crew, I might issue the required materials directly.
21.  As a warehouse manager, I need to approve the issued materials.

Enterprise Work Process for Materials Management

Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

1.  Any red/dashed process step in the diagram has associated precautions that should be carefully reviewed prior to implementation. These precautions

are italicized in the sections below, and the step number is referenced. Steps with rounded corners represent tasks that are automated by the software,
with required configuration.

2.  Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The Start and Repeat boxes in the diagram represent the

boundaries of the work process associated with this capability statement, rather than the start and end of a purely sequential engineering work process.

Short Description of Work Process

After the pipeline is created in Smart 3D or SmartPlant Isometrics, or any other supported modeling software, import the IDF or PCF file into SmartPlant
Spoolgen to add fabrication and erection data, such as field welds, detail sketches, and support details. When the data is ready to be shared, generate and
publish the spool drawings to SmartPlant Foundation. Retrieve the spool drawings into SmartPlant Construction to build work packages, import the parts lists
(BOM data) into SmartPlant Materials to manage the materials and import the piping data into Spoolgen Plus to create additional QA and welding
documentation and track the progress of piping.

Business Value



Industry standard deliverables – drawings and material reports – because SmartPlant Spoolgen is powered by Isogen

  SmartPlant Spoolgen and Spoolgen Plus work with piping data from almost any source – any system that uses Isogen and creates PCF or IDF files

  Scalable – SmartPlant Spoolgen and Spoolgen Plus have been used on the largest projects

  SmartPlant Spoolgen integrates seamlessly with suppliers and customers using the open, rule-based automation and reporting tools

  Eliminates most data copying / transcription errors by using automation

  Produces consistent, high quality drawings and other documentation for use in the fabrication shop and the construction site

  Highly productive – customers quote 10x productivity compared to drawing manually

  Simple to use and quick to learn

General Requirements

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.

  PBS structures must be consistent across the applications before integrating. When data in an application has been created in a non-integrated

environment, it is possible the PBS structure may not be compatible with requirements for integration. When this situation arises, restructuring of the
PBS is required.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.

Integration Capability Statements Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information





Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

  Refer to each product’s Compatibility Matrix on the Intergraph Smart Support web site for information on the compatibility of the versions of PP&M
products used in your environment, as well as with versions of operating systems, service packs, databases, and other pre-requisite products.

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

Integration Capability Statements Creating Pipe Spools for Procurement, Fabrication, and Construction

3

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

SmartPlant Spoolgen and Spoolgen Plus

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\SPO_Systems_Completion.pdf
---

SOLUTION SHEET

SMARTPLANT® ENTERPRISE FOR EPC
SYSTEMS COMPLETION SOLUTION

BENEFITS:

  Reuse of the engineering design basis to minimize the effort of gathering and maintaining data

and documentation.

  Automated allocation of checklists to eliminate manual allocation effort.

  Compilation of electronic completions dossiers.

  Out-of-the-box set of checklists or customers’ own lists can be configured.

  Offline completion of checklists on mobile tablet PCs.

  Graphical reporting of completion and punch list status on intelligent P&IDs and 3D models.



Library of standard status reports.

  Workflows to monitor, control and automate processes and distribution of work.

  Mobile viewing and redlining of documents

  Attach photo’s and videos in the field

•  Reuse of checklists for shutdowns, turnarounds, and outages.

Systems completion covers the process of taking a project from construction closeout to operations. It is arguably
the most critical and complex phase of the facility life cycle.

Every component of the facility must be checked to validate that it is built and installed correctly and functioning
properly. Systems and equipment are tested to ensure that they perform as expected. Any defects must be
recorded, repaired, and rechecked, and the process is tracked to ensure nothing is overlooked. Fluids and power
are introduced to bring the facility online.

Startup is known to be one of the riskiest aspects of operations and proper precautions must be followed. The
situation is complicated by the fact that construction is still ongoing while the other activities are taking place.
Since there are multiple system dependencies, any slippage in schedule will be magnified. Throughout this entire
process, there are many people involved from multiple companies. Responsibility is handed off at various points
along the way.

Managing systems completion well can reduce risk, shorten time to market, and improve time in production.
Proper planning, up-to-date statuses, accurate information, and well-coordinated activities are essential to
delivering the project on schedule.

Intergraph® SmartPlant® Enterprise for EPCs (SPE) Systems Completion (SC) Solution provides the tools you
need to help you be successful on your next project. SPE SC leverages the engineering design basis in the SPE
Core Solution that is built-up during the evolution of the plant design. This ensures an updated, complete, and
accurate basis for completions.

Mechanical completions and static commissioning check sheets and certificate templates are included to help
you get started. When the SPE SC Solution is used in conjunction with other SPE solutions, it provides a
seamless environment with access to all of the information needed to complete the work, without loading data or
scrambling to find current, complete, and accurate information.

SPE SC covers the complete systems completion workflow:

MECHANICAL COMPLETION – SPE SC offers a tool to create and manage check sheets for the items
to be inspected. A rules-driven process creates the proper types of check sheets for each item. The check sheets
are intelligent – data captured during inspections is automatically updated in the SPE SC database. Similarly, the
database is updated automatically to reflect the inspection status. Checksheets can be completed online or they
can be emailed or completed on a mobile tablet PC using the SPE Mobile solution that is available separately.
Walkdown checklists can be used to validate completeness before handover. Any defects that are noted result in
a punch list item so that the defect can be tracked to resolution. SPE SC creates certificates to document the
handover from one group to another. Optionally, you can seamlessly integrate the SPE Project Execution
Solution to track changes, interface items, site queries (RFI), and non-conformances. This provides a complete
history of not only the design but also the decisions that were made. Having all of the information readily
available reduces risk and wasted time when waiting for answers.

STATIC COMMISSIONING (PRE-COMMISSIONING) – During this phase, the construction,
testing, cleaning, and installation are verified prior to energizing equipment. SPE SC helps you manage these
verifications using check sheets that are automatically created and assigned using allocation rules. As in the
mechanical completion process, inspection and measurement information is captured electronically to automati-
cally update the SPE SC database. The software generates ready for commissioning certificates which include a
listing of any outstanding punch items and incomplete inspections.

DYNAMIC COMMISSIONING – The commissioning process ensures that the integrated components of
a system safely function as designed and that systems are commissioned in the most efficient sequence. SPE
SC automated processes generate notifications and pass the system through the proper review cycles. Status
visualization in the P&ID and 3D model as well as standard reports provide clarity of the commissioning progress.
Baseline measurements can be captured using check sheets so that the initial values are captured in SPE SC.
Ready for startup certificates can be created for handing over the system.

PUNCH LIST MANAGEMENT – Recording of defects and/or inspection non-compliance is essential for
tracking and planning their resolution. The type of problem recorded might necessitate immediate remediation for
a safety issue. But resolution might be delayed for something as simple as missing paint. Attaching a photograph
and/or video offers additional clarity about the defect. SPE SC generates lists of punch items and sends them to
contractors for resolution. A review process provides a means for verification. When certificates are created, a
listing of the outstanding punch items is included. This enables informed decision regarding the acceptance or
rejection of the handover offer.

PRE-STARTUP SAFETY REVIEW (PSSR) AND OPERATIONAL READINESS – This
process is essential to ensuring that all elements for a safe startup are in place. A team comprised of engineering,
operations, safety, and maintenance uses a process of checklists and walkdowns for verification. With the
risk-reducing measures functionality in SPE Core, the team can review the hazards and mitigating factors. They
can find the procedures and documentation within SPE SC to validate that all documentation is in place. Using
the design information in SPE SC, the team can see that the equipment and construction conform to design.
Electronic dossiers can be used to assemble the process safety management books. Then as each step is com-
pleted, a sign-off in the workflow is required before the review is complete. Managing the sign-offs in SPE SC
prevents overlooked steps in the process. Action Items can be assigned and tracked.

PRESERVATION – The high incidence of equipment failure during startup is often the result of improper
preservation. SPE SC manages preservation activities across the phases from site delivery until after the
equipment is in service. This ensures that there is no loss of continuity and all inspection records are maintained.
Users can create preservation check sheets to automatically capture the preservation results. A new preservation
is scheduled when the current one is completed. Reports indicate when preservation activities are upcoming or
overdue. Properly preserving equipment reduces the risk of failure during commissioning and startup. It also
maintains the warranty should a failure occur.

ELECTRONIC DOSSIERS – SPE SC uses an organized structure to manage the documentation related
to the completion process. These electronic books can be easily updated. Since the nature of SPE is that items
are stored only once, changes are greatly simplified. Costs associated with creating and maintaining hardcopy
dossiers can be averted by handing over the dossiers electronically.

Work Process for Engineering Project Handover

Introduction

  THIS IS AN INTERGRAPH INTERNAL DOCUMENT, WHICH SHOULD BE ONLY SHOWN TO CUSTOMERS OR PROSPECTIVE CUSTOMERS IN THE CONTEXT OF A PRESENTATION OR WORKSHOP WITH AN INTERGRAPH

TECHNICAL PERSON ALREADY KNOWLEDGEABLE ABOUT THE HANDOVER PROCESS AND THE SOLUTIONS DESCRIBED IN THIS DOCUMENT.
The Intergraph Work Process guides map the process, power, and marine industries’ basic work processes to SmartPlant Enterprise, including owner/operation solutions, and SmartMarine Enterprise solutions. This documentation helps your organization
transition from previous work practices to using Intergraph tools as the new way of executing projects, and includes:
Enterprise Work Processes to describe an entire engineering discipline or process at two different levels, including:
  Providing a swim lane diagram to define the typical roles and high-level processes involved in a project
  Showing how Intergraph tools are used to produce required deliverables
Integration Capability Statements to describe a specific work process among a set of Intergraph tools, including:
  Explaining the out-of-the-box capabilities of the tools and their recommended use
  Providing a swim lane diagram to show how the tools interact in the work process
  Stating critical requirements and precautions
Enterprise Work Processes and Integration Capability Statements can be customized and enhanced by your organization to create best practices that combine project expertise and software capabilities.

How to Use This Document

The Enterprise Work Processes contain diagrams organized into two levels.
The Level 1 diagrams represent the typical work process based on organization roles (disciplines), with a focus on the deliverables produced by that discipline and how that discipline interacts with other disciplines and external entities, such as vendors. At
this level, no specific tools are involved.
The Level 2 diagrams represent the sequence of major tasks performed using SmartPlant/SmartMarine Enterprise, and other tools, to produce each deliverable for that discipline. While there are multiple ways to produce a deliverable, the work processes
described in this document represent typical paths using SmartPlant/SmartMarine Enterprise (out-of-the box, or with minimum configuration) and do not include system customizations that could further enhance the work processes.

Reading Diagrams and User Stories

In some Level 1 and Level 2 diagrams, some steps have a black shadow which indicates the step is described in more detail in a separate work process diagram in this document. In some cases, a series of steps in a Level 1 diagram are grouped together
in a larger box to represent a subprocess that is described in more detail in another Level 1 or Level 2 diagram.
Because Level 1 diagrams represent disciplines or roles, they are represented using same color swim lanes. However, in Level 2 diagrams, SmartPlant Enterprise tools are represented by specific colors and non-Intergraph tools are represented in gray.
The swim lane diagrams should be read in conjunction with the user stories that follow each diagram for better understanding. Numbers in the diagram boxes correspond to the numbered steps in the user story. If a step has a red flag, this indicates
supporting information in an Integration Capability Statement, which is referenced at the bottom of the diagram, in the user story, and in the Appendix.
Most engineering work processes are not sequential, but instead are parallel and iterative. The numbers in the process steps do not necessarily indicate the strict order in which tasks must be accomplished. Although there is a general time sequence of
process steps from left to right on the diagrams, the sequence does not represent a strict timeline.
To view large diagrams on screen, you can use the Pan & Zoom feature in Adobe Acrobat by selecting View > Zoom > Pan & Zoom.

Printing the document
These documents must be printed using the landscape orientation to a printer that supports 11 in x 17 in (US tabloid) or A3 (297mm x 420 mm).

Contents

This document describes alternative strategies for the handover of the virtual plant asset from an EPC contractor to an owner operator (or from an EPC contractor to a Project Management contractor), and the role that the various complementary
technologies available from Intergraph can play in each. The virtual plant asset typically consists of a wide variety of information in many different formats: intelligent design data stored in SmartPlant databases, CAD files, drawings, documents, tag and
document registers, PDF files, spreadsheets, text documents, images, etc. While some owner operators have well-defined specifications and processes to manage the handover of the virtual plant, many do not. This document is intended to explain
alternative strategies for both interim (periodic) and final handovers of the virtual plant asset based on what deliverables are available from the EPC contractor, and the level of ambition the owner operator has with regards to intelligent management of the
virtual plant asset.
The processes in this document show the owner operator as receiving data into SmartPlant Enterprise for Owner Operators (SPO) which is Intergraph’s default recommendation for an owner operator based on the benefits that SPO offers in terms of:
pre-configured work processes; speed of deployment and reduced risk; simplified collaboration with EPCs that deploy equivalent SPE based solutions; and the out-of-the-box mappings provided in the SmartPlant Validation Transformation and Loading
(VTL) tool. The processes described are however equally applicable where an owner operator has already deployed SmartPlant Enterprise or a standalone SmartPlant Foundation installation.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Level 1 swim lane diagram:
  Engineering Project Handover

Level 2 swim lane diagrams:
  P1 - Handing Over Integrated Project Information
  P2 - Handing Over Non-Integrated Project and SmartPlant Foundation Information
  P3 - Handing Over Non-Integrated Project Information
  P4 - Handing Over Structured Project Information
  P5 - Handing Over Non-Structured Project Information
  A1 - Handing Over Integrated As-Built Information to an Integrated Environment
  A2 - Handing Over Non-Integrated and SmartPlant Foundation As-Built Information to an Integrated Environment
  A3 - Handing Over Non-Integrated As-Built Information to an Integrated Environment
  A4 - Handing Over Integrated As-Built Information to a Non-Integrated Environment
  A5 - Handing Over Non-Integrated and SmartPlant Foundation As-Built Information to a Non-Integrated Environment
  A6 - Handing Over Non-Integrated As-Built Information to a Non-Integrated Environment

Assumptions and Notes

Notes



Important notes about this document and the work processes it describes:

It is from the perspective of the owner/operator (O/O), not the EPC.

It specifically addresses a greenfield plant handover. However, many of the processes covered are also applicable to brownfield plants.

It does not address FEED handover.

It focuses on the exchange between one EPC and the owner/operator, not all of the possible handover scenarios. Additionally, it is not possible to merge multiple integrated EPC environments into one integrated owner environment; in other
words, separate tool databases (one from each EPC) will need to be maintained.
It shows SPO (SPO Core) at the owner's sites; however, the owner could implement SPO or SPF.


  Handover from EPC to construction is not addressed in this work process. That handover is addressed in Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning. Please refer to this document to learn more about the handover process

between the EPC and construction. To download, go to eCustomer, then to Product Documentation > Work Process Guides.

  Handover from EPC or O/O to commissioning is not addressed.
  Handover from owner project to owner operations occurs incrementally up until as-built handover, when operations receives the final plant.
Intergraph has a specification template for handing over enterprise data that mentions Intergraph products and is available at https://intergraph.box.com/s/c5fbedadb9b0671e8f1e. You must have an Intergraph account to access this site.


  While some amount of transformations are possible using SPE/SPO Handover, variances in the schemas between the originating EPC's SPE environment and the owner's SPO/SPF environment should be kept in mind and minimized as much as

possible.

  To result in an integrated enterprise environment on the owner side, you must ensure a consistent plant breakdown structure (PBS), tag naming, and so forth on the part of the EPC, even if the EPC is not using an integrated environment.

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

2

Roles

While this document focuses on the roles of the EPC and owner operator in the handover process, companies can play various roles in the engineering project handover, such as:
  Engineering contractor, including any of the following:


Internal team that manages the project
  Or both of the above

Acronyms

IFC – Issue for Construction
ITB – Invitation to Bid

  CMMS – Computerized Maintenance Management System
  EPC – Engineering, Procurement, and Construction
  EPCM – Engineering, Procurement, and Construction Management
  ERP - Enterprise Resources Planning
  FEED – Front End Engineering and Design


  O&M – Operations & Maintenance
  O/O – Owner/Operator
  PMC – Project Management Contractor
  PMT – Project Management Team
  SP – SmartPlant
  SPE – SmartPlant Enterprise (suite of Intergraph software products)
  SPF – SmartPlant Foundation
  SPO – SmartPlant Enterprise for Owner/Operators
  TIC – Total Installed Cost
  VTL – Validation, Transformation and Loading (Intergraph software product)

Product Descriptions

SPE/SPO Handover
There are two products discussed in this document with similar functionality, SPE Handover Solutionware and SPO Handover Solutionware. The first is for EPCs, and the second is for owner/operators. The functionality in both products is identical; only
the commercial licensing differs. Both parties involved in any handover, such as an EPC handing over to an O/O or an EPC handing over to a PMC, must have a license. SPE/SPO Handover Solutionware enables data and documentation to be quickly
and easily moved between SPF-based systems, including stand-alone SPF systems, SmartPlant Enterprise-based systems with integrated SmartPlant design tools, SmartPlant Construction, and SPO.
The solution supports the handover of intelligent 3D models and schematics that have been published from SmartPlant design tools into SPF. The Handover Solution preserves the inherent intelligence so that intelligent navigation in the source SPF
system is available on target SPF systems, as if it had been published locally. The Handover Solution supports both data and documentation that have been published from SmartPlant design tools as well as non-published data that has been authored in
SPF or loaded into SPF, such as vendor data and registers. The solution supports both periodic and final handover scenarios where data and documentation is scoped for handover, exported, and then imported into the target system.
Scoping of documents to include in an SPO Handover package is currently manual. For each periodic handover, the user selects the documents (or data) to include in the package. For example, the new document revisions since a certain date, such as
the date of the last handover

SPE/SPO Validation, Transformation, and Loading (VTL)
SPE/SPO VTL is a comprehensive solution capable of managing structured data import from multiple sources. VTL submits imported data to rigorous quality control before data extraction for loading into target systems. These systems can include
Intergraph’s SmartPlant Enterprise information management and engineering design tools or third-party applications. The import module reads incoming data (Excel or CSV files out-of-the-box, but can include XML, databases and other formats with
customization) and transforms this data using your preferred mapping and transformation. A pre-defined set of registers are available in SmartPlant VTL with out-of-the-box mappings to SPO. SPE/SPO VTL is available to both EPCs to be able to check
their deliverables quality prior to handover and to O/Os to check the quality of the information received.

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

3

Intergraph Proprietary Information

SmartPlant Fusion
SmartPlant Fusion is a solution to rapidly load existing drawings and documentation from the computer file system into the SPF system, including CAD files, laser scans, scanned PDFs, Word documents, and text files. Files are analyzed to extract title
block and tag information where possible, and create hotspots for navigation. Unlike SPE/SPO VTL, SmartPlant Fusion makes no attempt to validate the information.

Solution scenarios and matrices

The two matrices below help you understand the various solutions available from Intergraph and serve as an index to the Level 2 solutions diagrams in this document.
In both matrices, the two parties are referred to as the "handover provider" and the "handover recipient" because the same solutions are appropriate for various handover scenarios, including:
  EPC (provider) to PMC (recipient)
  EPC (provider) to Owner project management (recipient)
  EPC (provider) to Owner/Operator (recipient)
  PMC (provider) to Owner project management (recipient)
  PMC (provider) to Owner/Operator (recipient)
  Owner project management (provider) to Owner operations (recipient)
The above are common typical scenarios and other parties, such as Package Suppliers, EP Contractors, or Fabrication Contractors, can also participate in handovers. Depending on the project situation, where you see "handover provider" and "handover
recipient", you can substitute EPC, PMC, Owner/Operator, and so forth, as applicable.
Additionally, the solution diagrams can be used multiple times depending on the project situation. For example, let’s assume that 3 EPCs (EPC1, EPC2, and EPC3) need to hand over information to a PMC, and the PMC hands over information to the O/O.
In each of the following four cases, a handover takes place and represents a separate handover occurrence:
1.  EPC1 (provider) to PMC (recipient)
2.  EPC2 (provider) to PMC (recipient)
3.  EPC3 (provider) to PMC (recipient)
4.  PMC (provider) to O/O (recipient)

  The handover solutions used in each the four cases above might vary based on the work assigned to the providers and the technology solutions they use.

Example scenario
This example scenario explains how to interpret the two solutions matrices and how to determine which Level 2 diagrams and which software are required.
Using the scenario from Row #3 in Matrix 1 below, the handover recipient specifies full enterprise [SPO or SPF + SPE (Publish and Retrieve)], but the handover provider delivers less than specified [SPO or SPF + SmartPlant tools)]. In addition to this
intelligent data, handovers typically include other structured information (such as Excel lists) and non-structured information (such as PDFs or paper copies). The software solutions are:
1.  Looking at Row #3 in Matrix 1, the provider and recipient will use solution P2 for the periodic and IFC handovers and solution A2 for the As-Built handover. Now look at Matrix 2 to see which Level 2 diagrams describe solution P2 and solution A2, and

which software is required by the provider and the recipient.

2.  For the handover of structured and non-structured information in this example scenario, we need to look at Row# 5 in Matrix 1 and Row# 6 in Matrix 1, respectively. The provider and recipient will use solutions P4 and P5 for all types of handovers

(periodic, IFC and As-Built). Matrix 2 lists the corresponding Level 2 diagrams and the software required by the provider and the recipient.

Engineering Project Handover - Level 1 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

7

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Engineering Project Handover - Level 1 User Stories

  Dashed lines in the diagram represent an iterative communication process rather than the flow of data.

1.  As the corporate owner, I need to provide a set of requirements, specifications, and standards, including safety standards, quality assurance standards, and so forth to be used in the invitations to bid.
2.  As the corporate owner, I need to develop the front end engineering design (FEED) package so that I can develop the total installed costs (TIC) for the project and the processes involved in the plant.
3.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to prepare specific project requirements, including the handover specifications so that I can issue invitations to bid.
4.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to issue the invitation to bid (ITB), which includes FEED design information and handover specifications, to multiple EPCs.
5.  As an EPC invited to bid, I need to prepare a response to the ITB that addresses the project requirements.
6.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to negotiate and award the contract and pass along FEED data to the selected EPC(s).
7.  As the EPC, I need to design the plant and plan for the periodic deliveries, issue for construction (IFC), and final as-built milestones in order to provide information to the owner as specified in the contract.
8.  As the EPC, I need to hand over information periodically based on one of the milestones, such as the 30/60/90 percent completion deliveries or as specified in the contract. This process includes an iterative review and update with the owner’s project

management team (see step 10).

  See the solutions matrix to determine which solution meets your requirements.

9.  As the EPC, I need to hand over IFC information, as specified in the contract, so it can be delivered to construction. This process includes an iterative review and update with the EPC to inform them of any changes that occur during construction.

  See the solutions matrix to determine which solution meets your requirements.

10.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to review, comment, and approve all information (design, purchasing, and so forth) coming from the EPC. This process includes an iterative review and approval with comments.
11.  As a member of the owner’s plant operations team, I need to review and comment on the relevant information provided by the project management team.
12.  As a member of the owner’s plant operations team, I need to install and begin populating the operational systems (such as CMMS, ERP, spare parts, and so forth) and perform maintainability reviews.
13.  As part of the construction team, I need to receive information from the EPC to be used to construct the plant. During this process, I will be responsible for sending updated information back to the EPC for re-design, as well as all additional information

created during the construction process to be handed over to the owner.

  Handover from EPC to construction is described in the Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning document, which can be downloaded from eCustomer.

14.  As part of the commissioning team, I will be responsible for commissioning the plant. During this process, I will be responsible for sending additional information created during the commissioning process back to the EPC to be included in the updates

for the as-built handover.

15.  As the EPC, I need to create the as-built information so that it can be delivered to the owner. This process includes an iterative review and approve with comments with the owner’s project management team.

  See the solutions matrix to determine if this option meets your requirements.

16.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to review and approve with comments the as-built information from the EPC. I also need to review and assemble additional information from other sources, such as standards, and others,

and then confirm compliance with all relevant regulation standards.

17.  As a member of the owner’s plant operations and maintenance team, I need to complete the population of O&M systems.
18.  As a member of the owner’s plant operations and maintenance team, I need to create additional, plant-relevant documentation, including user manuals.
19.  As the owner operator, I operate the plant.
20.  As a member of the owner’s project management team, I need to accept the plant and make final payment after a warranty period.

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

8

P1- Handing Over Integrated Project Information - Level 2 Diagram

  All Level 2 diagrams illustrate the specific example of handover from an EPC (provider) to an O/O (recipient). However, the diagrams can also be used for other handover scenarios, as discussed in the Solution Scenarios section of this document,

where the roles are substituted, for instance EPC is replaced with PMC.

  All Level 2 diagrams show one O/O system consuming the handover in the bottom swimlane, but in some cases this may be broken down into two separate systems where a PMC, or the owner’s project management team, consumes the EPC
handover package and then evaluates what is actually required for the O&M system before making an additional handover to the operations department (not shown in the diagrams) which can potentially be performed using any of the Level 2
diagrams.

  Depending on your role as discussed above, where SPO Handover is listed in the user story, it could also be SPE Handover. Similarly, while SPO and SPF are explicitly mentioned, in certain cases they could be used interchangeably.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

9

P1- Handing Over Integrated Project Information - Level 2 User Stories

1.  As the EPC, I need to publish SmartPlant documents generated for the project.
2.  <automated process> SmartPlant Foundation loads published documents and data into the database.
3.  As the EPC, I need to supply other documents, such as CAD files, text documents, PDF files, spreadsheets, schedules, e-mail messages, and more that support the project and need to be included in the handover.
4.  As the EPC, I need to load project documents to SmartPlant Foundation using the relevant loading technology. In addition to loading the documents into SPF, I also have the option to process the data in the documents in one of the following ways:

  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data stored in the documents,
  Using some portion of SmartPlant Fusion to process the data within the loaded files, or
  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data in the documents before processing the data using portions of SmartPlant Fusion.

5.  As the EPC, I need to create other plant data required for the project, such as assets, disciplines, systems, check sheets, and so forth that will be included in the handover.
6.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to create the handover package by selecting the document versions and other information ready to be shared with the owner.
7.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to export (and transform as necessary) the handover package to the specified path on the system so that the owner can get it.
8.  As the owner/operator, I need to use SPO Handover to import (and transform as necessary) handover package files into the project system.
9.  As the owner/operator, I need to receive and consume the handover package, a process that might include loading, filtering, or processing data from the EPC before using it to populate the O&M system.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

10

P2 - Handing Over Non-Integrated and SmartPlant Foundation Project Information - Level 2 Diagram

  All Level 2 diagrams illustrate the specific example of handover from an EPC (provider) to an O/O (recipient). However, the diagrams can also be used for other handover scenarios, as discussed in the Solution Scenarios section of this document,

where the roles are substituted, for instance EPC is replaced with PMC.

  All Level 2 diagrams show one O/O system consuming the handover in the bottom swimlane, but in some cases this may be broken down into two separate systems where a PMC, or the owner’s project management team, consumes the EPC
handover package and then evaluates what is actually required for the O&M system before making an additional handover to the operations department (not shown in the diagrams) which can potentially be performed using any of the Level 2
diagrams.

  Depending on your role as discussed above, where SPO Handover is listed in the user story, it could also be SPE Handover. Similarly, while SPO and SPF are explicitly mentioned, in certain cases they could be used interchangeably.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

11

Intergraph Proprietary Information

P2 - Handing Over Non-Integrated and SmartPlant Foundation Project Information - Level 2 User Stories

1.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the files and information to be included in the handover package so that I can meet the handover requirements of the contract.
2.  <decision point> As the EPC, I need to determine if data will be validated by the owner using SmartPlant VTL before it is loaded into the project site.
3.  As the EPC, I need to create other plant data in my SmartPlant tools that will be included in the handover.
4.  As the EPC, I need to supply documents that support the project and need to be included in the handover. In cases where I have implemented SmartPlant tools in a standalone mode, these documents may include drawings and reports from those

tools, as well as such documents as CAD files, text documents, PDF file, spreadsheets, schedules, e-mail messages, and more.

5.  As the EPC, I need to load project documents to SmartPlant Foundation using the relevant loading technology. In addition to loading the documents into SPF, I also have the option to process the data in the documents in one of the following ways:

  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data stored in the documents,
  Using some portion of SmartPlant Fusion to process the data within the loaded files, or
  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data in the documents before processing the data using portions of SmartPlant Fusion.

6.  As the EPC, I need to create other plant data required for the project, such as assets, disciplines, systems, check sheets, and so forth that will be included in the handover.
7.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to create the handover package by selecting the document versions and other information ready to be shared with the owner.
8.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to export (and transform as necessary) the handover package to the specified path on the system so that the owner can get it.
9.  As the owner/operator, I need to use SPO Handover to import (and transform as necessary) handover package files into the project system.
10.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the data that will be processed by SmartPlant VTL during handover in a format that SmartPlant VTL accepts.
11.  As the owner/operator, I need to import the registers and project files for SmartPlant VTL. The EPC will send an e-mail message with the location from which to pull these files for import or a CD or disk that contains the files for import. I will import

these files into a SmartPlant VTL staging area.

12.  As the owner/operator, I need to run validation on files in the SmartPlant VTL staging area.
13.  <decision point> As the owner/operator, I need to run and view a report in SmartPlant VTL to determine if the data passed validation.
14.  As the EPC, I need to correct any data that did not pass validation so that it can be resubmitted to the owner (see step 10).
15.  As the owner/operator, I need to export the data received and transformed in SmartPlant VTL so that operations can get it.
16.  As the owner/operator, I need to load the data transformed in SmartPlant VTL.
17.  As the owner/operator, I need to receive and consume the handover package, a process that might include loading, filtering, or processing data from the EPC before using it to populate the O&M system.
