A1- Handing Over Integrated As-Built Information to an Integrated Environment - Level 2 User Stories

1.  As the EPC, I need to publish SmartPlant documents generated for the project.
2.  <automated process> SmartPlant Foundation loads published documents and data into the database.
3.  As the EPC, I need to supply other documents, such as CAD files, text documents, PDF files, spreadsheets, schedules, e-mail messages, and more that support the project and need to be included in the handover.
4.  As the EPC, I need to load project documents to SmartPlant Foundation using the relevant loading technology. In addition to loading the documents into SPF, I also have the option to process the data in the documents in one of the following ways:

Intergraph Proprietary Information

  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data stored in the documents,
  Using some portion of SmartPlant Fusion to process the data within the loaded files, or
  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data in the documents before processing the data using portions of SmartPlant Fusion.

5.  As the EPC, I need to create other plant data required for the project, such as assets, disciplines, systems, check sheets, and so forth that will be included in the handover.
6.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to create the handover package by selecting the document versions and other information ready to be shared with the owner.
7.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to export (and transform as necessary) the handover package to the specified path on the system so that the owner can get it.
8.  As the owner/operator, I need to use SPO Handover to import (and transform as necessary) handover package files into the project system.
9.  As the owner/operator, I need to set up the integrated SmartPlant environment.
10.  As the EPC, I need to create backups of the SmartPlant tool databases so that I can provide them to the owner.
11.  As the owner/operator, I need to restore the SmartPlant tool databases to my SmartPlant tool environment.
12.  As the owner/operator, I need to re-register the tools for integration and then republish to create a fully integrated environment in my project site.
13.  <automated process> SPO loads published documents and data into the database.
14.  As the owner/operator, I need to receive and consume the handover package, a process that might include loading, filtering, or processing data from the EPC before using it to populate the O&M system.

A5 - Handing Over Non-Integrated and SmartPlant Foundation Information to a Non-Integrated Environment - Level 2 User Stories

1.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the files and information to be included in the handover package so that I can meet the handover requirements of the contract.
2.  As the owner/operator, I need to set up the non-integrated SmartPlant environment.
3.  As the EPC, I need to create backups of the SmartPlant tool databases so that I can provide them to the owner.
4.  As the owner/operator, I need to restore the SmartPlant tool databases to my SmartPlant tool environment.
5.  <decision point> As the EPC, I need to determine if data will be validated by the owner using SmartPlant VTL before it is loaded into the project site.
6.  As the EPC, I need to supply documents that support the project and need to be included in the handover. In cases where I have implemented SmartPlant tools in a standalone mode, these documents may include drawings and reports from those

tools, as well as such documents as CAD files, text documents, PDF file, spreadsheets, schedules, e-mail messages, and more.

7.  As the owner/operator, I need to load the documents supplied by the EPC. In addition to loading the documents into SPO, I also have the option to process the data in the documents in one of the following ways:

  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data stored in the documents,
  Using some portion of SmartPlant Fusion to process the data within the loaded files, or
  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data in the documents before processing the data using portions of SmartPlant Fusion.

8.  As the EPC, I need to create other plant data required for the project, such as assets, disciplines, systems, check sheets, and so forth that will be included in the handover.
9.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to create the handover package by selecting the document versions and other information ready to be shared with the owner.
10.  As the EPC, I need to use SPE Handover to export (and transform as necessary) the handover package to the specified path on the system so that the owner can get it.
11.  As the owner/operator, I need to use SPO Handover to import (and transform as necessary) handover package files into the project system.
12.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the data that will be processed by SmartPlant VTL during handover in a format that SmartPlant VTL accepts.
13.  As the owner/operator, I need to import the registers and project files for SmartPlant VTL. The EPC will send an e-mail message with the location from which to pull these files for import or a CD or disk that contains the files for import. I will import

these files into a SmartPlant VTL staging area.

14.  As the owner/operator, I need to run validation on files in the SmartPlant VTL staging area.
15.  <decision point> As the owner/operator, I need to run and view a report in SmartPlant VTL to determine if the data passed validation.
16.  As the EPC, I need to correct any data that did not pass validation so that it can be resubmitted to the owner
17.  As the owner/operator, I need to export the data received and transformed in SmartPlant VTL so that operations can get it.
18.  As the owner/operator, I need to load the data transformed in SmartPlant VTL.
19.  As the owner/operator, I need to receive and consume the handover package, a process that might include loading, filtering, or processing data from the EPC before using it to populate the O&M system.

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

28

A6 - Handing Over Non-Integrated As-Built Information to a Non-Integrated Environment - Level 2 Diagram

  All Level 2 diagrams illustrate the specific example of handover from an EPC (provider) to an O/O (recipient). However, the diagrams can also be used for other handover scenarios, as discussed in the Solution Scenarios section of this document,

where the roles are substituted, for instance EPC is replaced with PMC.

  All Level 2 diagrams show one O/O system consuming the handover in the bottom swimlane, but in some cases this may be broken down into two separate systems where a PMC, or the owner’s project management team, consumes the EPC
handover package and then evaluates what is actually required for the O&M system before making an additional handover to the operations department (not shown in the diagrams) which can potentially be performed using any of the Level 2
diagrams.

  Depending on your role as discussed above, where SPO Handover is listed in the user story, it could also be SPE Handover. Similarly, while SPO and SPF are explicitly mentioned, in certain cases they could be used interchangeably.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Engineering Project Handover - INTERNAL ONLY

29

Intergraph Proprietary Information

A6 - Handing Over Non-Integrated As-Built Information to a Non-Integrated Environment - Level 2 User Stories

1.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the files and information to be included in the handover package so that I can meet the handover requirements of the contract.
2.  As the owner/operator, I need to set up the non-integrated SmartPlant environment.
3.  As the EPC, I need to create backups of the SmartPlant tool databases so that I can provide them to the owner.
4.  As the owner/operator, I need to restore the SmartPlant tool databases to my SmartPlant tool environment.
5.  <decision point> As the EPC, I need to determine if data will be validated by the owner using SmartPlant VTL before it is loaded into the project site.
6.  As the EPC, I need to supply documents that support the project and need to be included in the handover. In cases where I have implemented SmartPlant tools in a standalone mode, these documents may include drawings and reports from those

tools, as well as such documents as CAD files, text documents, PDF file, spreadsheets, schedules, e-mail messages, and more.

7.  As the owner/operator, I need to load the documents supplied by the EPC. In addition to loading the documents into SPO, I also have the option to process the data in the documents in one of the following ways:

  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data stored in the documents,
  Using some portion of SmartPlant Fusion to process the data within the loaded files, or
  Using SmartPlant VTL to validate the data in the documents before processing the data using portions of SmartPlant Fusion.

8.  As the EPC, I need to prepare the data that will be processed by SmartPlant VTL during handover in a format that SmartPlant VTL accepts.
9.  As the owner/operator, I need to import the registers and project files for SmartPlant VTL. The EPC will send an e-mail message with the location from which to pull these files for import or a CD or disk that contains the files for import. I will import

these files into a SmartPlant VTL staging area.

10.  As the owner/operator, I need to run validation on files in the SmartPlant VTL staging area.
11.  <decision point> As the owner/operator, I need to run and view a report in SmartPlant VTL to determine if the data passed validation.
12.  As the EPC, I need to correct any data that did not pass validation so that it can be resubmitted to the owner (see step 8).
13.  As the owner/operator, I need to export the data received and transformed in SmartPlant VTL so that operations can get it.
14.  As the owner/operator, I need to load the data transformed in SmartPlant VTL.
15.  As the owner/operator, I need to receive and consume the handover package, a process that might include loading, filtering, or processing data from the EPC before using it to populate the O&M system.

Translating and sharing 3D models with SmartPlant Interop Publisher

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Integration Capability Statements Translating & Sharing 3D Models with SmartPlant Interop Publisher

1

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Description and Business Value
The SmartPlant® Interop Publisher application provides a single solution to handle various 3D data formats and output model data (graphics and properties)
for display and use in Intergraph SmartPlant Review, Intergraph Smart™ 3D, SmartPlant Construction, and SmartPlant Foundation applications. For a list of
supported source formats, please refer to the Supported Source Formats section in this document.

After creating a site and a project in SmartPlant Interop Publisher to organize all the models that will be translated, define the configuration settings: project
settings, translation settings for the graphics and customization of the property label display. You must register the project with the corresponding SmartPlant
Foundation plant if the translated model will be used with SmartPlant Foundation or SmartPlant Construction. Steps 2 through 4 are one-time administrative
tasks.

From one of the many 3D software applications supported, including Intergraph’s CADWorx, PDS and Smart 3D, generate the required 3D source files in the
applicable format, including all or only the part of the plant you want to include in the output. Add the source files to the project and translate the files, which
results in the Smart Model that can be used with the Intergraph applications. If needed, you may optionally transform the resulting Smart Model (moving,
rotating and/or scaling the model), or you can perform this step later in SmartPlant Review, Smart 3D, or SmartPlant Foundation application themselves.

You can provide the Smart Model to each target location as required, such as for joint venture partners.

In SmartPlant Interop Publisher, you can launch SmartPlant Review to open and review the Smart Model.

In SmartPlant Review, you can open the Smart Model and review the model as usual.

In Smart 3D, you attach the Smart Model as a reference to the Smart 3D model to view it and model against it. You can:

  Attach multiple Smart Models from different sources as Reference 3D Models to your Smart 3D model.



Inspect and provide property views for the referenced model objects.

  Filter objects from referenced models based on their type and properties.

  Extract drawings using graphic and label rules, showing relative positioning of objects from referenced models with limited annotation.

Before publishing to SmartPlant Foundation, if you have custom properties that you wish to include in the translated model, you must map them using the
Schema Editor. After the data (model) is published, you can view it in SmartPlant Foundation as usual, or retrieve in SmartPlant Construction to use
components from the resulting Smart Model in the construction work packages.

On the second and subsequent translations of a Smart Model, repeat steps 5, 7 and optionally 8, then:







If the Smart Model is attached to Smart 3D, it will be necessary to update the Reference 3D Model in step 11;

If the Smart Model is published to SmartPlant Foundation, republish with steps 13 and 14;

If the Smart Model is used by SmartPlant Construction, in addition to steps 13 and 14, you also need to retrieve it again with step 16.

Business Value

You can use data from different sources such as PDS, PDMS, Tekla, AutoCAD, or MicroStation files, and use the resulting model in Intergraph applications.
SmartPlant Interop Publisher supports generic industry standards, such as Building Information Management (BIM), including Industry Foundation Classes
(IFC), CIS/2, and others.

Integration Capability Statements Translating & Sharing 3D Models with SmartPlant Interop Publisher

2

Intergraph Proprietary Information

SmartPlant Interop Publisher provides solutions for business cases such as the following:

  Supports disconnected workshare

Because the data is translated to files at the source site and attached as a reference at the target site, live connection between the databases is not
required. Periodic publish and update of the Smart Models allows multiple partners to work on a joint venture project.

  Allows for better security in joint venture projects

Network connectivity is not required. The translated files can be transferred using any appropriate mechanism.

  Allows you to protect your intellectual property

Share only what you want your partners or subcontractors to see by filtering specific sections of the plant or object properties you want to include or
exclude from model data that is shared.

  Provides the ability to work with a wide range of datasets from Intergraph and non-Intergraph tools

Model data from other formats such as .att (from PDMS) and XMpLant can be translated to the format recognized by SmartPlant Enterprise applications.
This allows for heterogeneous tools to be used on a single project.

  Supports parallel design for increased productivity

If a project has several similar units, only one unit has to be modeled and translated. This translated unit can then be attached multiple times with the
required positioning.

  Allows for better use of survey data from laser scanning for construction and as-built projects

The converted 3D model can be viewed when displaying laser data in the SmartPlant Enterprise applications.

General Requirements

  Smart 3D applications require a license of SmartPlant Interop Publisher for conversion and reference of all formats.

  Laser scanning applications require the SmartPlant Review Point Cloud module to support the display of laser data inside SmartPlant Review.

  Applications must be registered with SmartPlant Foundation prior to performing publish and retrieval operations.



Item Tagging must be consistent across the enterprise in order for correlation and retrieval to function properly.

  Smart 3D and SmartPlant Interop Publisher use only revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an integrated environment. SmartPlant P&ID,

SmartPlant Electrical, and SmartPlant Instrumentation use either revisions managed by the tool or revisions managed by SmartPlant Foundation in an
integrated environment.





Integration is not supported in a heterogeneous internationalization environment.

Integration is not supported with applications in Project/As-built mode.

  Special configuration considerations are required when using workshare in conjunction with integration. For implementations, including combinations of

workshare, handover, and integration, please consult the SmartPlant Enterprise Support organization.

Integration Capability Statements Translating & Sharing 3D Models with SmartPlant Interop Publisher

3

  Check the validity and correctness of the data from both internal departments and external partners before publishing documents, to ensure the same

standards are used. Use both the data integrity functions provided in each tool and other quality checks to resolve data integrity issues.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Publish and Retrieve Considerations



In a SmartPlant integrated environment, publishing a document is not synonymous with issuing a document. Publishing is for sharing data between
engineering disciplines, prior to completing the design and issuing the document.

  When publishing, you have the option of using the auto-correlate workflow, which loads the published document and data into the SmartPlant

Foundation database, compares specific object data from different sources, and automatically correlates representations of the same object from
different tools.

  Publishing a document with the PublishForLoadOnly workflow allows you store the data in SmartPlant Foundation so that it can be reviewed and
compared with data from other tools. However, when published using this workflow, a document cannot be retrieved into other authoring tools.









In the early phases of design, when data is very preliminary, publishing and retrieving should be infrequent. As design progresses, you may consider
publishing on a regular schedule, for example every two weeks, and as you approach any milestone, you should consider increasing the frequency of
publish and retrieve operations.

In general, disciplines that create documents that are heavily relied upon by downstream disciplines, such as the Process discipline and its P&IDs,
should consider the impact of their changes to downstream applications and make an additional unscheduled publish as a result of significant changes.

If a document is undergoing major revisions that are not completed in time for a scheduled publish, the publishing of that document should be delayed
until the revisions are completed, to prevent unnecessary changes in the downstream applications.

If the catalog or reference data has recently been updated, you should ensure that the design data has been synchronized with the updated catalog or
reference data prior to publishing.

  Before publishing any document, a new revision can be obtained from SmartPlant Foundation, if required. If a new revision is not required, simply

publish the document.

  Publishing can be configured to send an email notification automatically using a SmartPlant Foundation workflow, which can send the email to a list of
recipients by document type. Alternatively, the user can select an email notification workflow at actual publish time. In this way, the email recipient is
alerted that a document is available for retrieval.

  Documents can be published to any owning group available in SmartPlant Foundation. To retrieve a document published with an owning group, a user
must have permissions configured in SmartPlant Foundation to allow them to retrieve documents in that owning group. This feature could be useful in a
multi-EPC environment.

ADDITIONAL NOTES

SmartPlant Interop Publisher enables you to work with file types from a variety of data sources, as shown in the tables below. If your authoring tool can
output any of the industry standard graphic and data formats listed below, or if your authoring tool can export data to an Intergraph generic DGN/DRV
format, SmartPlant Interop Publisher can translate your source files.

Authoring tools can output 3D modeling information to graphics and data files, or they can output to a single graphics file that contains the three-dimensional
geometry information. When you add source files in SmartPlant Interop Publisher for translation, select only the graphics file, but make sure the graphics
and data (if any) files reside in the same folder. The Source Files column in the table below lists the graphic and data file type combinations that can be
used.

SOLUTION SHEET

BENEFITS:

•	Out-of-the-box,	user-friendly	processes
that	can	be	rapidly	adjusted	and	de-
ployed	at	low	risk.

•	Capture,	retrieval,	and	analysis	across
the	project	life	cycle	for	key	project
management	processes.

SMARTPLANT® ENTERPRISE FOR
OWNER OPERATORS PROJECT
EXECUTION SOLUTION

Managing projects to ensure delivery on schedule, to specification, and within bud-

get is a major challenge for owner operators and project management contractors

(PMCs). The increasing size, complexity, and globalization of projects mean that tradi-

•	Tight	integration	with	affected	plant

tional ways of managing projects are no longer sufficient.The Intergraph® SmartPlant®

objects	of	the	plant	design	basis	in	the
SPO	Core	Solution	that	enables	the
potential	impact	of	these	processes	to
be	analyzed.

•	Common	environment	for	undertaking

these	associated	work	processes.

•	Powerful	management	reporting	for

overall	visibility,	enabling	proactive	inter-
vention	and	better	decision-making.

•	Automated	and	flexible	workflows	en-

suring	consistent	adherence	to	project
procedures,	demonstrable	compliance,
and	complete	auditable	traceability.

•	Reduced	risk	of	project	cost	or	schedule
impact	from	inadequate	management
of	project	interfaces,	change,	non-
conformity,	or	queries.

•	The	ability	to	link	project	execution

processes	to	each	other.	For	example,
a	technical	query	arising	from	a	site
can	result	in	a	temporary	or	permanent
non-conformity	or	a	project	change	that
could	impact	an	interface	item.	The	abil-
ity	to	link	these	project	execution	work
processes	provides	complete	control	and
traceability.

Enterprise for Owner Operators (SPO) Project Execution Solution builds upon the SPO

Core Solution and provides pre-configured processes supporting key work processes

for successfully managing the execution of greenfield and brownfield projects. These

processes include:

•	Management of change.

•	Management of technical/site queries (Requests for Information).

•	Management of nonconformities.

•	Interface management.

Traditional methods of managing these processes through spreadsheets or electronic

archive solutions do not give sufficient visibility or control of these critical project manage-

ment processes. The SPO Project Execution Solution offers management reports to focus

attention and help avoid impact on project schedule and cost. The solution uniquely pro-

vides close links between these processes and the underlying engineering design basis to

facilitate fast impact analysis across these processes.

MANAGEMENT OF CHANGE IN PROJECTS
Changes to the approved project design basis are the single greatest factor to influ-

ence project cost and schedule. Any major CAPEX project will be subject to thou-

sands of changes and hundreds may be under consideration at any one time. The

process of evaluating changes is complex, involving many technical and administrative

stakeholders in the project, and the complexity is compounded by overlapping scopes

between changes.

The SPO management of change process provides a unique level of change control within

projects, and provides management with increased visibility through management report-

ing. This includes the critical distinction between development within existing project scope

(commercial and design development) and changes to existing project scope (commer-

cial and design changes). Changes are referenced to the underlying engineering design

basis items affected: tags, documents, and parts of the Plant Breakdown Structure. This

enables analysis of what other changes either already approved for implementation or

under review may be impacted by the change under consideration and which interface

items would potentially be affected if the change should be imple-

mented. The SPO management of change process ensures auditable

traceability through the review, approval, and implementation cycle for

changes using automated workflows to demonstrate compliance and

adherence with project authorization matrices and procedures.

MANAGEMENT OF NON-CONFORMITIES
Non-conformities to applicable laws, regulations, corporate governing

MANAGEMENT OF TECHNICAL AND SITE QUERIES
On any major project, thousands of technical and site queries (also

known as Requests for Information) need to be addressed and an-

swered within a tight schedule to avoid impacting project schedule

and potential variation orders. Traditional paper-based or electronic

archive-based solutions demand a high level of administration

and manual effort between all of the parties involved in resolving

documents, and project specifications all need to be closely managed

queries.

on projects. Traditional electronic archive or paper-based systems

suffer from poor process management and reporting. The associated

history of review and approvals can be difficult to find, especially dur-

ing operations. When an incident does occur on a plant with such a

system, it can take a long time to gather the necessary information

for the investigatory team and receive permission from the regula-

tory authorities to resume production.

The SPO non-conformity process is closely linked and integrated

with other SPO project execution processes, such as technical

queries or management of change, and demonstrates compliance

with regulatory requirements for managing non-conformities. SPO

offers a process to manage non-conformity requests from all

parties and the granting of temporary and permanent waivers or

deviations. The SPO non-conformity process provides an auto-

mated workflow to track the process of receiving, reviewing, and

approving non-conformities.

The SPO non-conformity process includes the linking of non-confor-

mities to affected plant areas, systems, tags, documents, etc. which

are affected. This helps make non-conformities highly visible for the

operations readiness team so corrective steps can be taken, such

as increasing the frequency of planned inspection or maintenance.

The technical/site query process in SPO greatly simplifies the admini-

stration and processing of queries. Flexible, templated workflows

and management reports ensure consistent handling. Bottlenecks

can be identified early to enable management to implement reme-

dial action before claims arise.

INTERFACE MANAGEMENT
Interface management provides technical interface control between

project stakeholders such as the owner operator project team, the

owner operator corporate organization, any PMC, EPC, contrac-

tors, package suppliers, authorities, and many others. A typical

CAPEX project will have 20 to 100 separate interfaces that need

to be managed, each with hundreds of interface issues that need

to be resolved. The traditional use of spreadsheets is insufficient

for managing the complexity of interfaces and reporting. SPO’s

templated process offers hierarchical structuring of interface

needs and manages the identification, responsibility, planning,

and status of both physical and soft interface items. Interface

issues and information needs can be linked to the affected parts

of the plant. Tags, areas, systems, and more provide multiple

access routes to interface information. Management reporting

highlights where interface issues are not being addressed as

Where incidents do occur, SPO reduces downtime impact on the

planned. This enables management to take remedial measures

plant and facilitates a quicker restart of production by enabling all

before consequences become serious and reduce the risk of

documentation and information related to the waiver process to be

claims and disputes.

presented without delay, including a complete, auditable traceability.

# SOURCE: vendor_hexagon\SmartPlant Enterprise for EPCs\WorkfacePlanning.pdf
---

Construction - Define and Develop Work Packages - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Construction - Define and Develop Work Packages - Level 2

1.  As a construction planner, I need to create or update the work packages, with information such as work package number; the related construction work package from the schedule; and contractors, disciplines, purpose, status, and area from project

configuration information.

2.  As a construction planner, I need to schedule each work package with planned start and finish dates and estimated man hours.
3.  As a construction planner, I can provide additional details of the scope of the work packages, such as any special circumstances, so that it is available to the workforce.
4.  <automated process> Design data is issued to construction planning - 3D model, P&ID drawings, spool drawings, and so forth.
5.
(subprocess) As a construction planner, I need to retrieve the design data from the SmartPlant Foundation Engineering site into SmartPlant Construction. For more information see the handover scenario diagrams described later in this document.
6.  As a construction planner, I need to add the components to the work packages so that the work package is fully defined with all requirements to complete the work. These components are related from the 3D model data, P&ID drawings, and so forth,

or created as manual components.

7.  As a construction planner, I can add drawings from design tools for reference, if needed.
8.  As a construction planner, I can add fabrication drawings for reference, if needed.
9.  <automated process> As the components are associated with the work package, the rules of progress are applied automatically by SmartPlant Construction to determine the actual work steps and the planned man hours.
10.  As a construction planner, I can attach the vendor documents for reference, if needed.
11.  As a construction planner, I can attach standards and specification documents as supplied by the owner, if needed.
12.  As a construction planner, I need to refine and validate the work packages. This may include adjusting work steps, printing and validating the work package, attaching snapshots from the model, and so forth.

Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning

1

Construction - Determine the Work Package Constructability - Level 2 Diagram

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Construction - Determine the Work Package Constructability - Level 2

1.  As a construction planner, I need to define a time frame that represents a portion of the construction so that the software identifies the appropriate work packages to include in the simulation based their planned start and finish dates.
2.  As a construction planner, I need to perform a 4D (of a model or a drawing) simulation so that I can review the sequence of the work packages within that time frame to ensure constructability.
3.  <decision point> As a construction planner, I need to watch the simulations to determine if the order of the work packages is acceptable or if any occur out of sequence (such as columns being placed prior to foundations being installed).
4.  As a construction planner, to address any encountered conflicts, I need to redefine the sequence of the work packages, move components from one work package to another, or delete the components from the work package. Snapshots can be used

to document any conflicts encountered during the 4D simulations and highlight the conflicts encountered. See the work process Construction - Define and Develop Work Packages - Level 2 Diagram (on page 1) for more information.

5.  As a construction planner, I need to select a work package to review in SmartPlant Review and use its animation features to review installation paths of one or more components.
6.  As a construction planner, I need to select the specific components for animation.
7.  As a construction planner, I need to apply the timeline and create the installation path.
8.  As a construction planner, I need to review the animation of the installation path of the included components to ensure the most efficient installation sequence.
9.  <decision point> As a construction planner, I need to determine if the animation illustrated any conflicts that need to be addressed.
10.  As a construction planner, I need to send the component sequence from SmartPlant Review to SmartPlant Construction.
11.  <automated process> The component sequence is updated automatically using data from SmartPlant Review.
12.  As a construction planner, I need to attach the SmartPlant Review video to the work package so that the work crew has a visual aid to complete the installation of the work package.
13.  As a construction planner, I need to check if the materials required for the work package will be available by the planned start date of the work package by running a forecast in SmartPlant Materials.
14.  <automated process> SmartPlant Materials builds a forecast, based on the drawing added to the work package, to check if the material is in inventory or available based on upcoming shipments. The results are returned to SmartPlant Construction.

  If you are using SmartPlant Materials and have configured it to work with the delivered SmartPlant Construction API, this report is created automatically in SmartPlant Materials through a command in SmartPlant Construction. For any other

material management applications, an API is provided and additional customization is required.

15.  <decision point> As a construction planner, I need to determine if the material is available when it is needed based the forecast results returned by SmartPlant Materials.
16.  As a construction planner, to address material shortages, I need to redefine the sequence of the work packages, move components from one work package to another, or delete the components from the work package. See the work process

Construction - Define and Develop Work Packages - Level 2 Diagram (on page 1) for more information.

17.  As a construction planner, based on the material availability from the forecast, I need to run a reservation in SmartPlant Materials to ensure the materials are allocated for the work package.

Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning

2

18.  <automated process> SmartPlant Materials reserves the materials based on the forecast and availability.
19.  <decision point> As a construction planner, I need to determine if all other constraints (such as equipment, tools, permits, and crews) are met.
20.  <decision point> As a construction planner, I need to determine if the constraints can be resolved.
21.  As a construction planner, to address any unresolvable constraints, I need to redefine the sequence of the work packages, move components from on work package to another, or delete the components from the work package. See the process

Construction - Define and Develop Work Packages - Level 2 Diagram (on page 1) for more information.

22.  As a construction planner, I need to resolve the remaining constraints.
23.  As a construction planner, I need to prepare the work package for issue so that construction can begin.

Intergraph Proprietary Information

Enterprise Work Process for Construction Work Package Planning

3

VALIDATION, TRANSFORMATION,
AND LOADING (VTL)

A SmartPlant® Enterprise and SmartPlant Enterprise for Owner Operators
(SPO) Solution

STREAMLINE DATA HANDOVER FROM PROJECTS AND
BROWNFIELD DATA TAKE-ON
The handover of data from CAPEX projects and plant turnarounds is a formidable task.

It can involve the transfer of thousands of documents and millions of individual data items

from suppliers and contractors to the owner operator. The challenge is how to quickly and

efficiently perform the following tasks:

•	Check the quality of information being transferred

•	Keep records of which checks have been performed and the results obtained

•	Load approved information into target systems for operations

On large CAPEX projects, it is not unusual for this process to cost in excess of US$15

million and take up to a year.

The take-on of data from brownfield plants and validation in preparation for plant revamps or

•	Automated	capture	of	test	results.

for loading into data systems requires that data from multiple sources (systems, files, and

•	Microsoft®	Windows®	Workflow

hardcopy documentation) be gathered, transformed, loaded, consolidated, and validated.

Foundation	for	managing
work	processes.

•	Powerful	export	mechanism	to
load	data	into	target	systems.

The Intergraph® Validation, Transformation, and Loading (VTL) solution is designed to meet

these needs and lower the cost of project handovers and data take-on.

THE SOLUTION
Intergraph VTL is a comprehensive solution capable of managing

data import from multiple sources. VTL submits imported data to

rigorous quality control before data extraction for loading into target

systems. These systems can include Intergraph’s SmartPlant Enterprise

information management and engineering design tools or third-

party applications.

ADVANCED IMPORT CAPABILITIES
VTL empowers you to choose your preferred mapping and trans-

formation tool. You can use any mapping tool capable of generating

•	PL/SQL and DLL rule definitions for more complex rules.

•	Unit of measure validation.

•	Mandatory fields validation.

•	Cascading errors – Errors on one object can create errors on

associated objects.

You can add your pre-defined rules to rule sets that are run against

future data submissions.

FLEXIBLE STAGING AREA
VTL provides a highly flexible staging area into which you can map

XSLT, such as MapForce from Altova, to map incoming data or export

and load incoming data submissions from contractors, suppliers, or a

data to be loaded into target systems.

legacy data system. Once in the staging area, you can validate data

The import module reads incoming files and transforms these using

the XSLT generated from a mapping tool. The import module’s

against your pre-defined rule sets. The results from testing are

retained in the staging area. Reports then list the results.

advanced features can:

•	Prompt you for input.

GENERIC EXPORT MODULE
The VTL export module facilitates data filtration and export. The module

•	Automatically calculate values based on incoming data

also supports the initiation of loading programs to load data into

where needed.

Intergraph SmartPlant Enterprise software or third-party applications.

RFC 4180 compliance.

USER-DEFINABLE RULES
VTL allows you to quickly and easily define your own rules. It supports

BUILDING ON PROVEN EXPERIENCE
AND TECHNOLOGY
Intergraph has extensive, in-depth experience in helping customers

manage the handover of data from complex projects to operations,

an extensive range of rule types, including:

as well as helping successfully migrate data from legacy systems.

•	Syntax rules.

•	Uniqueness validation.

•	Relationship cardinality.

•	Date and time validation.

•	Integer/float validation.

•	String and pick list validation.

We use this rich legacy of experience as the basis for the design

of our comprehensive VTL tool set. VTL is based on Intergraph’s

industry-leading SmartPlant Foundation information management

technology to ensure a highly robust and flexible platform for data

staging and validation.

need existing data

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

1.    SUMMARY

The conditions under which today’s increasingly complex process plants are engineered
and constructed require increased efficiencies in their design, engineering and
construction activities.  This places increasing demands on the supporting infrastructure,
engineering tools and work processes needed to coordinate plant design in a more
collaborative environment across multiple sites aimed at reducing project lead times and
design costs.

One way to reduce lead time in design is to subcontract all or some portion of the work to
external partners ‐ at both design, fabrication and construction phases.  This solution
affords flexibility in workforce utilization within the enterprise compared to the
alternative of hiring additional resources but with the added advantage of increasing
potential cost savings.  With increasing shortages in skilled engineering resources world‐
wide, alternative ways to undertake process plant design has lead to the introduction of
technologies which support true collaborative engineering.

The technology enabler for such an environment is a design philosophy and infrastructure
which supports concurrent engineering processes whereby it is possible to manage
globally distributed projects from a single, centralized location without using excessive
additional efforts in administration, coordination and project control.  Today, this is the
area in which workshared concurrent engineering practices and supporting 3D plant
modeling software tools are being used to support more complex project execution
strategies and where SmartPlant 3D’s workshare capabilities are now having increasing
business impact to the benefit of both plant owners and engineering organizations.

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

2.    Terminology

The following terminology is used in this white paper:

•  Global Workshare Configuration (GWC):  refers to the concept of distributing the
responsibility for aspects of a plant project’s design and/or construction across
physically separate sites.

•  Site:  a database server at some physical location, designated as either a host or

satellite.

•  Permission Group (PG):  a mechanism to control access (i.e. the read and/or

write) to objects within the database.

•  Replication:  the means by which information at one site is synchronized with

information at another site.

•  Business Object (BO):  refers to a software component that represents a real‐

world application object (e.g. a plate, a piece of equipment, a pipe, a structural
member, a cable tray system, etc.), encapsulating the business rules, logic, and
controls necessary to ensure it behaves in a consistent and predictable manner.
•  Host Location:  the first site created in a GWC that controls the replication and

synchronization process.

•  Satellite Location:  one or more remote sites created in a GWC that contains

copies of the data on the host location.

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

3.

Introduction

As part of a growing world‐side community of Intergraph customers, Suncor Energy has
been an influential user and integral partner with Intergraph Process Power & Marine in
the implementation of SmartPlant 3D, which has been designed to support the vision of
highly concurrent, scalable, globally distributed design.  SmartPlant 3D is architected on
commercially available, open relational database technology that takes advantage of
highly reliable remote data replication.  This enables both plant owners and its
Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) companies to support a process of
working globally with multiple design centers in distributed networks.  In practical terms,
SmartPlant 3D provides a workshared design environment supporting the sharing of the
3D plant model in geographically distributed sites based on consistent workflows through
control of user permissions.

The following white paper will present a summary of the business drivers for this
requirement, an overview of the issues facing such an approach, an outline the details of
the implementation, highlight a production success story and lessons learned, and
summarize the advantages achieved using this workshared approach.

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

4.    Business Drivers

The business innovation for the plant industry that is made possible by workshare
technology is one of the most promising to have emerged in recent years.  Process plant
design and construction across all key sectors is cyclical, characterized by fluctuating
demand tied to raw material and production capacity.  The ability to compete and survive
hinges on the ability to specialize on some aspect of the overall process, whether that is
producing the overall plant, or only a specific area, system, subsystem, or component.  As
a result, the ability to isolate some subset of the design data and temporarily or
permanently assign responsibility for it from one group to another, while maintaining
the data integrity in an integrated product model in a concurrent design environment, is
now increasingly being adopted in the process plant industry.  This philosophy of working
– Global Workshare ‐ is also now being adopted in related industries, for example
offshore and shipbuilding where the boundaries between design, fabrication and
construction continue to blur.

As noted in the terminology section, Global Workshare refers to the concept of allocating
and distributing the responsibility for some aspect of a plant’s design and/ or construction
among physically separate sites while ensuring safe, reliable and consistent information
exchange without the risk of disruptions to day‐to‐day business operations.  Examples of
this include:

(cid:131)  delegating design responsibility for some subset of a plant given to a sub‐contractor

by a prime contractor;

(cid:131)  giving responsibility for inter‐discipline design tasks among design departments of an

enterprise which are based in different locations, cities, states, or countries;

(cid:131)  giving responsibility for approving the overall design and integrity of the plant;

(cid:131)  giving responsibility for the fabrication and construction by outside subcontractors or

by other facilities under the control of the enterprise.

Not all business cases or drivers involve outside subcontractors or separated geographies.
Global Workshare can be, and is used to support scalability and optimization in highly
concurrent design scenarios within large enterprises.  Working processes, procedures and
associated workflows need to be developed and/ or adapted to take into consideration
such factors as data access permission levels that can be assigned to individual users and/
or groups of users.  Through use of “permissions”, design management can control what
data can be created or changed by whom in this distributed digital domain.  These types
of controls are not new to process plant design; they exist in some form or another in
every design office today.  However, they are generally not formalized and structured in a
manner that allows them to be exploited by today’s information systems.

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

4.1  Use Case Scenarios

While the business drivers outlined above may be broadly applicable across
multiple industries like Automotive, Aerospace, Construction, Process, and
Power, it should be emphasized that advocating a “one size fits all” solution is
not necessarily applicable to all.  Differences in product structure and
configuration influence the scale and nature of implementations.

The process plant sector has a number of unique characteristics associated with
large, one‐of‐a‐kind complex facilities that cannot be addressed with generic
off‐the‐shelf tools.  Factors such as regional vendor and supplier bases,
scalability, integrated design and associated fabrication and construction
processes, and management of large capital assets all combine to necessitate
the need for highly specialized tools developed specifically for the industry.  It is
important to make the clear distinction between the need for tools tailored to
and for the specific needs of an industry segment, and the use of technology in
the underlying architecture of the tool that applies to a broader market
segment.  In the case of SmartPlant 3D ‐ the ‘tool’ ‐ and Global Workshare ‐ the
‘technology’ ‐ just such an approach has been adopted.  The following section
outlines a number of common scenarios typical of the process plant industry
and highlights characteristics and issues that present peculiar challenges in a
Global Workshare environment.

4.1.1  Design Agent Scenario

This scenario is characterized by a relatively low number of client users
working with a high volume of evolving design models and interacting
with a varied and ad hoc set of external consultants and subcontractors.
The design timeframe is relatively short, ranging from several weeks to 3‐
4 months.  The modeling activities are primarily concentrated in the
“design” stage of the plant lifecycle and limited if any effort is expended
in the detailing and construction stages.  The deliverables are primarily
drawings and reports suitable for conveying design intent, concept, and
estimating cost and schedule.  The level of detail, need and use of
standardized details and rate of change are relatively low.  The amount
of data reuse is extremely high, as is the need for rich catalogue content.

4.1.2  Intra‐Enterprise Centralized Design, Distributed Fabrication Scenario

In this scenario, the number of design clients is very high, the volume of
designs is low, and the interaction with subcontractors limited.  The
design timeframe is on the order of 3‐6 months.  The modeling activities
span the design, detailing, and construction stages of the product
lifecycle.  Deliverables are design and production drawings, reports, and
data used to support plate/ profile cutting, pipe bending, and robotic
welding.  Because this scenario is within a single enterprise, it is possible

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

7

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

to tightly control the IT infrastructure, processes and procedures, and
project execution.  The projects in this scenario are typically more
complex and the design level of detail, the duration of the design cycle,
and the need to provide versions of the deliverables result in a high level
of change that must be managed across all the design stages.  The need
for design standardization, automation, and rich catalogue content is
high.  This is typical of today’s large offshore projects.

4.1.3  Inter‐Enterprise Distributed Design and Fabrication Scenario

What makes this scenario different from Scenario 4.1.2 above is the de‐
centralization of the design and fabrication activities.  In addition to the
physical logistical problems this entails, controlling and managing the
project is complicated by the existence of disparate IT infrastructures
and processes and procedures.  Access control, model synchronization,
standardization, format and content of deliverables, and engineering
change management become much more important and difficult,
requiring more sophisticated and specialized tools and techniques.

4.1.4  Intra‐Enterprise Highly Scalable Design Scenario

This is a specialized case of Scenario 4.1.3 above where the focus is on
optimizing and fine‐tuning database configurations using workshare to
balance the load between servers where the design environment is
highly concurrent and interactive.

4.1.5  Owner/Operator Lifecycle Asset Management Scenario

This scenario is still relatively untapped in the process plant Industry
today and can be characterized as an integrated “product model”
delivered to the owner operator in the form of an “as‐built” database.
The characteristics unique to this scenario are the challenges it presents
to the IT infrastructure with respect to database synchronization; the
extended level of detail of catalogue content (vendor content, spare
parts, technical operating manuals, etc.); and the duration of the
operation stage of the lifecycle—extending over many years dependent
upon the nature of the plant.  The design level of detail and volume of
change slows markedly from that during the design and fabrication/
construction stages and is relatively low except for periods when the
plant undergoes planned, scheduled maintenance.

Client access to the design stage data and catalogue on the other hand, is
extremely high and most likely needs to be supported on mobile clients.
Uses of this data include operator training, plant inspection, support,
maintenance, and repair.

5.

Infrastructure

Database replication, while supported by functions and features in the application, is not
undertaken within SmartPlant 3D directly.  Instead, SmartPlant 3D relies on features
already included as an integral component available from the database vendor.  A full
discussion on the topic is outside the scope of this paper and is left to literature in the
public domain (see reference [1]).  However, from a high‐level perspective, it involves
servers playing the roles of “publisher”, “distributor”, and “subscribers”.  The publisher is
the server or database that sends its data to another server.  The distributor is the server
or database that manages the flow of data through the replication system, and the
subscribers are the servers or databases that receive data.  The role of distributor may be
combined with that of the publisher on the same server.  In the context of this paper, the
term ‘site’ is synonymous with publish and subscribe servers—each of these would be a
separate site that would be synchronized via database replication.

There are different types of replication, namely “snapshot”, “transactional”, and “merge”.

Snapshot replication is the simplest and works by copying all replicated data from the
publisher database to the subscriber’s databases on a periodic basis.  It is best in
situations where the size of the data is not very large and the data changes infrequently.

Transactional replication captures all changes and stores “insert”, “delete” and “update”
statements in the distributor database.  These changes are then sent to the subscribers
and applied in the same order as they were made.  This type of replication is best for
frequently changing data and large data sets.  This is the model most commonly
employed with SmartPlant 3D in the plant industry.

Merge replication is the most complicated and captures all incremental data changes in
the source and target databases and reconciles conflicts according to user‐configured
rules.  It is best used to support autonomous changes of data on the publisher and
subscriber servers and databases.

Given the global nature of today’s plant user, replication and work sharing offers the
potential to efficiently and effectively manage an on‐shore or offshore project among
several design teams, consultants, sub‐contractors, and/or fabrication yards.

The following section outlines some of the necessary infrastructure to support global
workshare.

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

Figure 2: Database Configuration

From a conceptual user viewpoint, the system can be described as a single, virtual
database solution.  The physical implementation, however, makes use of multiple
databases ‐ more or less transparent to the designer using SmartPlant 3D—for the
purposes of effectively and efficiently segregating the information.  In order to
properly setup and utilize Global Workshare, the database administrator must
understand the databases that SmartPlant 3D uses.  Figure 2 above graphically
depicts these databases, which include:  Catalogue;  Catalogue Schema;  Model;
Site;  and Site Schema.

The Catalogue database contains reference data, which includes part dimensions
and industry standards.  The Catalogue database is the sum total of the non‐
graphical information derived from the reference data (catalogue content) delivered
with the system.  The delivered reference data can be modified or custom reference
data can be added/ used to create a new Catalogue database for a specific project.

The Model database contains all instances of parts in the physical representation of
the model.  The Catalogue and Model databases share the same schema.  A plant
design project (i.e. an on‐shore or offshore facility) utilizes all three databases
together: Catalogue database, Catalogue schema database, and Model database.

The Site database and schema are containers for the other databases.  The Site
database stores work‐breakdown and user access for the model.  A Site database
can have multiple Model and Catalogue databases.  Typically, there is one Site
database set for each customer location.

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

10

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

The Global Workshare Configuration (GWC) allows sharing all the data within one
plant project with remote sites.  Designed for companies running projects from
multiple sites (EPC’s or Owner/Operators, for example), or for multiple companies
that are working on a single plant project, the Global Workshare functions involve a
single, central database in which all the changes come together as if they were
created at the same site.  Pivotal in the sharing of data within a workshare
environment are the geographical hubs known as locations.  Two types of locations
are required to share model data among multiple sites: a Host location and one or
more Satellite locations.

The Host location is a set of one or more database servers on a local area network
(LAN) that contains the original set of databases associated with a site.  The Satellite
location is a set of one or more database servers on a LAN that contains the
replicated database associated with a site.  The Host location is created
automatically and as such, the Host location is the first location created.  Satellite
locations, on the other hand, are created manually.  Once they are created,
locations can be associated with permission groups and models as part of the
workshare replication process.  In the global workshare solution, data sharing
between different locations is achieved through real‐time Model database
replications of the entire plant at all Satellite locations.  The Catalogue and
Catalogue schema databases and the Site and Site schema databases are
maintained on the Host server while Satellite locations have read‐only replications
of these databases.

For the Catalogue, Catalogue Schema, Site, and Site Schema, the Host should
control the data.  The other machines are Satellites of the Host.  The Host is a
Publisher of Catalogue, Schema, and Model data to the Satellites and a Subscriber of
Model data from the Satellites.  The Satellites are Subscribers to the Host for
Catalogue, Schema, and Model data.  They are Publishers of Model data to the Host.
In small and midsize configurations, the database server is usually its own
distributor.  However, in the case of large configurations or multiple smaller
configurations, it is possible to use a dedicated distributor server.

Consolidation is the process of merging back all the replicated databases on the
different Satellite servers to the databases on the Host server so as to form a single
database of each type.  The resulting merged databases resemble the original
databases; users can work with them as if the databases were never replicated or,
at some later point in the design process, the databases can be replicated again
with either the same or with different satellite locations.  The ability to easily grow
the workshare configuration by creating additional satellite locations after the
project start as demand dictates and then to just as easily shrink the configuration
by consolidating databases provides the ultimate flexibility in meeting the
fluctuating demands of the industry.

While this setup is overhead that must be undertaken in a GWC, it is something that
is typically done once at the start of the project and then run autonomously during
the project execution.

SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

5.2  Configuring Global Workshare

Projects intending to use Global Workshare need to properly configure the
hardware and software based on a number of factors, such as:
•  The number of concurrent users per site;
•  The number of sites;
•  The size of the project (which translates into the size of the databases);
•  Other software that is running on the machine.

The following section provide a more detailed discussion of these factors.

5.2.1  Server Hardware Requirements

At the time of writing, the following specifications are adequate for a typical
production‐level database server supporting 30 ‐ 40 concurrent users:
•  4x Dual Core Processor
•  16 GB RAM
•  100 BaseT or higher network interface
•  Digital tape or DVD backup system for server
•  CD‐ROM drive access, either locally or through a network connection

For optimum performance, the server should be configured with multiple
hard drives.  The size of the hard drive is not as important as the speed and
15K RPM or faster is recommended.  Further disk arrays may be added to
maximize performance and avoid I/O bottlenecks.  RAID 10 is recommended
for performance and redundancy.

The following example shows a typical configuration for a small project using
SQL Server 2005.  Three disks is the recommended minimum:
•  C: Drive – Operating System and SQL Server Software
•  D: Drive – Database Files
•  E: Drive – Database Log Files

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

12

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

A configuration for a large project using SQL Server 2005 is detailed  below:
•  C: Drive (Raid 1) ‐ 2 hard drives internal to the server for the operating

system and Page File

•  E: Drive (Raid 10) – 8 SAN drives for the data file for the Model database
•  F: Drive (RAID 10) – 4 SAN drives for the Model log file
•  G: Drive (RAID 0 or 1) – 2 SAN drives for the temp database
•  H: Drive (RAID 10) – 4 SAN drives for the Catalogue, Site, Site Schema,

•

Catalogue Schema, Report, and Report Schema data files
I: Drive (RAID 1) – 2 SAN drives for the Catalogue, Site, and Schema log
files
J: Drive (RAID 1) – 2 SAN drives for the Master database data

•
•  K: Drive (RAID 1) – 2 SAN drives for the log file

5.2.2  Concurrent Users

The size of the system depends partly on the number of concurrent users,
that is, users actively working at the same time.  In a GWC, it is probable that
work will be done at several sites in a non‐concurrent way.  In this case, there
is less impact on performance.

As an example, if we consider two sites with 60 users at each site but the
users at the two sites do not work at the same time, the user load would be
60 users.  In a GWC when users are working concurrently at several sites, the
work done at one site will impact each site as the data is replicated to the
other sites.  In a “hub and spoke” configuration (where a single hub server is
designated as the distributor and the spokes are all satellite servers), the data
is first pushed to the hub and from there, pushed to the other sites—
effectively increasing the user load (i.e. the “equivalent user load) on the
servers (see figure 3).  Determination of the equivalent user load is based on
the number of actual users on the server plus a factor applied to the total of
concurrent users of all the other sites.  For example, the equivalent user load
would be less for a configuration of 6 sites with 40 users each where only 3
sites would be working concurrently—say because of time‐zone differences—
than it would be for a configuration where all 6 sites would be working
concurrently.

5.2.3  Project Size Estimates

The model database is an important factor in determining project size, but it
is not the only factor.  In addition to the explicit design clients, there are
remote and batch processes for such things as generation of deliverables
(drawings, reports, manufacturing output, etc.), licensing, and “interference
detection” that run as additional clients and increase the user load on the
server.  Characteristics for three prototypical project scenarios (small,
medium, and large) are provided below:

Small Project Scenario:

•  1 to 15 effective users on one server
•  Model database up to 8 GB
•  Server used for model and catalogue databases and catalogue file

server

•  Separate interference checking server (or workstation)
•  Separate batch server (or workstation)
•  Name generator server and licensing server (or workstation)
•  Modeling and Administrator workstations can be separate or they can

use the same workstation

Medium Project Scenario:

•  16 to 50 effective users on one server
•  Model database 8 – 15 GB
•  Databases and catalogue file server
•  Separate interference checking (IFC) server (or workstation)

•  Separate batch server (or workstation)
•  Name generator server and licensing server (or workstation)
•  Modeling and Administrator workstations can be separate or they can

use the same workstation

•  Separate distribution server for GWC

Large Project Scenario:

•  51 to 100 effective users on one server
•  Model database 15 GB or more
•  Databases and catalogue file server (have a separate catalogue file
server for multiple large projects or plant designs sharing the same
catalogue)

•  Separate interference checking (IFC) server (or workstation)
•  Separate batch server (or workstation)
•  Name generator server and licensing server (or workstation)
•  Modeling workstations
•  Administrator workstation
•  Separate distribution server for GWC

5.3  Access Control

Access control is addressed through the concept of “Permission Groups” (PG’s) that
play several key roles in the system.  At the most basic level, they enable segregation
and control of design responsibility, while at the most advanced level they enable
partial backup and restore; form natural boundaries for change propagation; and are
the backbone of multi‐site distributed design (database replication and global work
sharing).  Every Business Object (BO) created in SmartPlant 3D belongs to one and
only one PG.  The system creates a default PG, however, users are free to create as
many additional PG’s as are necessary to control their design process.  Within each
SmartPlant 3D task, the user selects the PG to be used for the creation of new
objects.  The means are also provided to enable a user to easily modify the PG of an
existing object.  A PG serves as a “logical” grouping of BO’s within the model
database.

Access control in SmartPlant 3D is rule‐based.  Access rights are defined by a list of
access rules and granted to a user or a group (as defined by the operating system).
The currently supported access rights are “read”, “create”, “update”, “delete”, and
“approve”.  Access control is orthogonal to data stores, that is, several data stores
(catalogue and /or model databases) can support the same PG, and hence an access
rule will apply to all these data stores.  In general, Access Control is not intended to
replace security.  Rich clients (the client workstations on the network used by
SmartPlant 3D designers and engineers to model and interact with the model) have
direct access to the database and security is managed by using the security features
of the database vendor.  When an application or a web server hosts services, access
control may be used as a security feature.

Intergraph Process Power & Marine 2008
SmartPlant®3D Global Workshare White Paper

15

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

PG’s serve as the basic mechanism for partitioning data to support distributed design
through database replication and work sharing between two or more sites.  A given
site has “read‐only” access to the data of the other sites.  Two sites cannot have
“write” access at the same time to the same data.  It is better when the partitioning
of data does not change frequently, for example, a site is given “read/write” access
in the morning to some data and another site is given the “read/write” access to the
same data in the afternoon.   The reason being that the partitioning of data needs to
be controlled at a central location by an administrator because changing the
partitioning is a heavy administration operation and requires that the data stores at
every other site be put “offline” while the changes are made.

As with the project setup for database servers in a GWC, setting up the proper PG’s is
something that is typically done once at the beginning of the project.  Determining
the proper PG’s takes some time and thought, however, once done, it can be used as
the basis for future projects where only minimal changes need to be made to address
changing team players or roles.

5.4  Data Interoperability

Global workshare projects based on homogenous software applications and tools can
avoid having to deal with interoperability issues stemming from the need to
exchange and share data.  This is increasingly important where product data are
exchanged among stakeholders in the broad process.  Designers, suppliers,
customers, subcontractors and others must be assured of timely access to the
relevant data with full security and integrity.

The emergence of newer data exchange protocols such as CIS/2 and XMpLant based
around specific ISO Standards continued to have a positive business impact on the
ability to share data among today’s plant design stakeholders.  However, the absence
of consistent standards across all plant disciplines means that factors such as open
architectures, the availability of floating software license configurations, hardware
costs, ease of use, and training requirements will be significant discriminators in
technology solutions.  Products like SmartPlant 3D based on commercial, relational
database technology and an open architecture offer a proven, practical path to
address this important business need.

6.    SmartPlant 3D Global Workshare in Production – User Case Study

6.1  Introduction

Up to this point, a description and discussion on the technical issues underlying the
adoption and use of technology to facilitate global workshare has been provided.
The following case study highlights a real implementation using workshare
technology on a production project.

6.2  Suncor Energy – ‘Firebag’ Oil Sands Project, Canada

Suncor Energy Inc. is Canada’s first commercial oil sand operator and the second
largest oil sand producer world‐wide.  With estimated oil sand reserves in excess of
15 Billion barrels, a major capital investment program to support the extraction and
processing of the oil sands is now underway in Fort McMurray region north of
Calgary.  Early in this program, Suncor realized that basic technology limitations
inherent in their existing design engineering and modeling tools posed a potential
risk to their ability to reach their strategic goal aimed at raising the current
production rate to 550K barrels/ day by 2012.

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

To mitigate this risk, Suncor undertook an evaluation of all major engineering design/
3D plant modeling solutions.  In 2006, Suncor selected Intergraph’s SmartPlant
Enterprise suite of tools as their strategic solution of choice for all future capital
expenditure (CAPEX) programs.  SmartPlant Enterprise was first deployed on
Suncor's Firebag Stage 3 Steam Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD) development,
which is now in the detailed design phase (see Figure 7.).

Firebag Stage 3 is a substantial project involving two engineering firms workshared
across five locations (sites) spanning three countries and two continents.  Today, over
300 concurrent users are accessing a single replicated SmartPlant 3D multi‐discipline
plant model across these sites with virtually instantaneous replication of all data
between all the workshared sites.

Figure 7:  SmartPlant 3D rendered image showing
Firebag Stage 3 Steam Assisted Gravity Drainage (SAGD)

Beyond Firebag Stage 3, SmartPlant Enterprise has now been further deployed on the
Firebag Stage 4 SAGD program.  This has now entered the Front End Engineering and
Design (FEED) phase and builds on the same Stage 3 model.

Workshare was identified as a strategically important reason why Suncor chose to go
to SmartPlant 3D.  Suncor knew that the level of work that was to take place on all the
Stages of Firebag (3, 4, 5 and 6) meant that there literally wasn’t sufficient
engineering capacity available in Calgary to handle the scale and complexity of the
overall program.  Workshare across multiple sites allowed the effective deployment of
experienced engineering resources globally across the internationally dispersed sites.

Integrated Product Development using Global Workshare

7.    Conclusions

In conclusion, Intergraph’s current product strategy supporting global workshare
addresses the complex business drivers influencing the need for organizations to balance
the demands that complex, time‐critical projects place on increasingly scarce engineering
resources.  With the flexibility provided by dynamic, distributed, virtual workforces,
engineering data can now be successfully shared on a global scale in a transparent,
seamless workshare configuration.  The technology and infrastructure needed to support
such working arrangements and environments is mature, robust, and fully supports
today’s increasingly complex capital projects.

From a project size/ scalability standpoint, it is clear Intergraph’s product strategy
supports practical project scenarios and configurations typical of today’s plant industry
ranging from small, design‐only based projects to large, globally distributed inter‐
enterprise projects offering tangible business benefits across all described project
scenarios.  One of the key lessons learned from Suncor Energy’s Firebag project is that in
a global workshare environment, workflow and engineering change management are
critical factors to achieve overall implementation success.
